questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Consider the following:
Assertion (A): Under Article 368, the Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution.
Reason (R): The Parliament is the Supreme Legislative body elected by the People of India.
Code:
questions.questionText: Consider the following: Assertion (A): Under Article 368, the Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution. Reason (R): The Parliament is the Supreme Legislative body elected by the People of India. Code:

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are correct, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are correct, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Assertion (A)** states that under **Article 368** of the Indian Constitution, the **Parliament** can amend any part of the Constitution.
This is correct as **Article 368** grants the **power of amendment** to the **Parliament**. **Reason (R)** states that the **Parliament** is the **Supreme Legislative body** elected by the **People of India**.
This is also correct as the **Parliament** is the highest legislative body in India and is elected by the people through **democratic elections**.
**Reason (R)** is the **correct explanation of Assertion (A)** because the fact that the **Parliament** is the supreme legislative body elected by the people of India gives it the authority to amend the Constitution through **Article 368**.
The Constitution vests this power in the **Parliament** as a reflection of the **democratic principles** and **representative nature** of the Indian polity.
**

Important Tips
- **Article 368** is a provision in the Constitution of India that deals with the power of **Parliament** to amend the Constitution and its procedure.
- It is in **Part XX** of the Constitution and states that **Parliament** can amend any provision of the Constitution by way of addition, variation or repeal, subject to some conditions and limitations.
- Some amendments require the **ratification** of not less than **one half of the States** by resolution passed by their Legislatures before they are presented to the President for assent.
- **Article 368** also declares that nothing in **Article 13**, which prohibits laws that abridge or take away any of the **fundamental rights**, shall apply to any amendment made under this article.

statementLines: The word 'Hindu' in Article 25 of the Constitution of India does not include-
questions.questionText: The word 'Hindu' in Article 25 of the Constitution of India does not include-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Article 25** of the Constitution of India is a **fundamental right** that guarantees the **freedom of conscience** and the right to freely profess, practice, and propagate **religion** to all persons.
**Explanation II** to **Article 25 (2) (b)** states that In sub-clause (b) of clause (2), the reference to **Hindus** shall be construed as including a reference to persons professing the **Sikh, Jaina or Buddhist religion**, and the reference to **Hindu religious institutions** shall be construed accordingly.
Therefore, among the given options, only **Parsis** are not included in the word **Hindu** in **Article 25** of the Constitution of India. **

Important Tips-The Constitution does not define the term **minority** but it recognizes **religious and linguistic minorities** under **Article 30(1)**.
- The Constitution also provides various **rights and safeguards** for the protection and promotion of the interests of minorities.
Some of them are:
- **Article 29**: It grants any section of citizens having a distinct **language, script or culture** the right to conserve the same and the right to be protected against **discrimination** on any ground.
- **Article 30**: It grants any religious or linguistic minority the right to establish and administer **educational institutions** of their choice and the right to receive aid from the State without discrimination.
- **Article 350A**: It imposes a duty on every State and local authority to provide adequate facilities for instruction in the **mother tongue** at the primary stage of education to children belonging to linguistic minority groups.
- **Article 350B**: It provides for a **Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities** appointed by the President to investigate all matters relating to the safeguards for linguistic minorities and to report to the President upon those matters.
- **Article 347**: It empowers the President to direct any State to recognize any language spoken by a substantial proportion of the population of that State as an **official language** of that State

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: In which Article of Indian Constitution Doctrine of Due Process of Law is included?
questions.questionText: In which Article of Indian Constitution Doctrine of Due Process of Law is included?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The phrase **due process of law** is **not included** in any of the articles of the Indian Constitution.
However, the concept has been enshrined in **Article 21** of the Indian Constitution, which states that No person shall be deprived of his **life or personal liberty** except according to the **procedure established by law**.
Over time, the **Supreme Court of India** (**Maneka Gandhi vs Union of India case (**1978**)**) has interpreted **Article 21** to include the doctrine of **due process of law**, which means that not only there must be a law to deprive a person of his life or personal liberty, but also that the law must be **fair, just, and reasonable**.
The **Supreme Court** has held that **Article 21s** reference to **procedure established by law** has been understood as **due process of law**

statementLines: Under Article 356 of the Constitution of India, President's Rule was imposed for the first time in-
questions.questionText: Under Article 356 of the Constitution of India, President's Rule was imposed for the first time in-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **Presidents Rule** under **Article 356** of the Constitution of India was for the first time imposed in the year **1951** in the former state of **Punjab and East Punjab States Union (PEPSU)**.
**

Important Tips
- **Article 355** imposes a duty on the Centre to ensure that the government of every state is carried on in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution.
- It is this duty in the performance of which the Centre takes over the government of a state under **Article 356** in case of failure of **constitutional machinery** in state.
- This is popularly known as **Presidents Rule**. It is also known as **State Emergency** or **Constitutional Emergency**.
- The **Presidents Rule** can be proclaimed under **Article 356** on two grounds-one mentioned in **Article 356** itself and another in **Article 365**:
- **Article 356** empowers the President to issue a proclamation, if he is satisfied that a situation has arisen in which the government of a state cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution.
- **Article 365** says that whenever a state fails to comply with or to give effect to any direction from the Centre, it will be lawful for the president to hold that a situation has arisen in which the government of the state cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following is correct?
questions.questionText: Which of the following is correct?

options.a: The Nehru Report (1928) had advocated the inclusion of Fundamental Rights in the Constitution of India.
options.b: The Government of India Act, 1935 referred to Fundamental Rights.
options.c: The August Offer, 1940, included the Fundamental Rights.
options.d: The Cripps Mission 1942 referred to Fundamental Rights.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.

Option a is correct: The **Nehru Report** was a memorandum drafted by a committee chaired by **Motilal Nehru**, with **Jawaharlal Nehru** acting as the secretary, to appeal for a new **dominion status** and a **federal set-up** of government for the constitution of India.
It also proposed for the **joint electorates** with **reservation of seats for minorities** in the legislatures. The report demanded inalienable **fundamental rights** for the people of India, such as **equal rights for women**, **right to form unions**, and **universal adult suffrage**.
Option b is incorrect: The **Government of India Act,**1935**did not refer** to **Fundamental Rights**. It only provided for **provincial autonomy** and a **federal structure** of government, but it did not guarantee any civil liberties or rights to the people of India.
Option c is incorrect: The **August Offer, 1940**, **did not include** the **Fundamental Rights**. It was a proposal made by the British government to the Indian leaders to seek their cooperation during the **Second World War**.
It offered some concessions such as expansion of the **executive council**, establishment of a **constituent assembly** after the war, and recognition of the principle of **federation**.
Option d is incorrect: The **Cripps Mission**1942**did not refer** to **Fundamental Rights**. It was an attempt by the British government to negotiate with the Indian leaders and persuade them to support the British war effort.
It offered some proposals such as granting **dominion status** to India after the war, setting up a **constituent assembly** to frame a new constitution

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution guarantees equality of opportunity to all citizens of India in matters relating to public employment?
questions.questionText: Which of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution guarantees equality of opportunity to all citizens of India in matters relating to public employment?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 16** of the Indian Constitution is a **fundamental right** that guarantees **equality of opportunity** for all citizens in matters relating to **employment or appointment** to any office under the State.
It prohibits **discrimination** on the grounds of **religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, residence** or any of them.
- **Article 16 (1)** states the general principle of **equality of opportunity** for all citizens in matters relating to employment or appointment to any office under the State.
- **Article 16 (2)** specifies the grounds on which **discrimination is prohibited**, namely religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, residence or any of them.
- **Article 16 (1)** and **16 (2)** together guarantee **equality of opportunity** to all citizens of India in matters relating to **public employment**
- **Article 16 (3)** deals with **residence requirement**, **Article 16 (4)** deals with **reservation for backward classes**, and **Article 16 (5)** deals with **religious or denominational institutions**.
**

Important Tips **Article 16** of the Indian Constitution has six clauses. They are:
- **Clause (1)**: **Equal opportunity** for all citizens in public employment or office.
- **Clause (2)**: **No discrimination** on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, residence or any of them in public employment or office.
- **Clause (3)**: **Parliament** can make laws on **residence requirements** for certain public posts within a State or Union territory.
- **Clause (4)**: State can make provisions for **reservation** of appointments or posts for **backward classes** who are under-represented in the services under the State.
- **Clause (4A)**: State can make provisions for **reservation in promotion** with **consequential seniority** for **scheduled castes and scheduled tribes** who are under-represented in the services under the State.
- **Clause (4B)**: State can treat **unfilled reserved vacancies** of a year as a **separate class of vacancies** to be filled up in any succeeding year or years without exceeding the **50% reservation limit**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which one of the following is incorrect?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is incorrect?

options.a: Fundamental Duties are part of Fundamental Rights.
options.b: Fundamental Duties are not a part of Fundamental Rights.
options.c: Fundamental Duties are enumerated in Part-IV A of the Indian Constitution.
options.d: Article 51 A explains the duties of every citizen of India.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Fundamental duties** are **not part** of the **fundamental rights**. The **fundamental rights** are the basic rights guaranteed to the citizens and the aliens by the Indian Constitution under **Part III**.
- They are **enforceable by the courts** and can be **suspended during an emergency**.
- The **fundamental duties** are the **moral obligations** of the citizens to promote the spirit of **patriotism** and preserve **Indias unity**.
- They are listed in **Article 51A** under **Part IV-A** of the Indian Constitution.
- They are **not enforceable by any court** and **cannot be suspended** at any time

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: The First Constitution Amendment Bill passed in 1951 was related to
questions.questionText: The First Constitution Amendment Bill passed in 1951 was related to

options.c: Protection of agrarian reforms in certain States

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **First Constitution Amendment Bill** passed in **1951** was related to several changes to the **Fundamental Rights** provisions of the Indian constitution, including the **validation of zamindari abolition laws** in certain States.
The amendment sought to secure the **constitutional validity** of these laws, which were challenged in courts by landowners on the ground of being discriminatory or violating the **right to property**.
**

Important Tips-The **First Constitution Amendment** added a new **clause (4)** to **Article 31**, which stated that no law made by the State for the acquisition of any estate or of any rights therein or for the extinguishment or modification of any such rights shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by **Article 14, Article 19 or Article 31**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: By
Which one of the following Constitutional Amendments Delhi became 'National Capital Region'?
questions.questionText: By Which one of the following Constitutional Amendments Delhi became 'National Capital Region'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **69th Amendment** of the Constitution of India, officially known as **The Constitution (Sixty-ninth Amendment) Act, 1991**, designated the **Union Territory of Delhi** as the **National Capital Territory of Delhi** and provided for a **legislative assembly** and a **council of ministers** for Delhi.
**

Important Tips Features of **69th Constitutional Amendment Act**:
- It conferred a **special status** to Delhi by declaring the **Union Territory of Delhi** as the **National Capital Territory of Delhi** and provided for a **legislative assembly** and a **council of ministers** for Delhi.
- It inserted two new articles, **Article 239AA** and **Article 239AB**, in the Constitution, which deal with the **special provisions** for Delhi as the **National Capital Territory** and the provisions in case of **failure of constitutional machinery**.
- It designated the administrator of Delhi appointed under **Article 239** as the **Lieutenant Governor** and made him/her bound by the **aid and advice of the council of ministers** except in matters where he/she is required to act in his/her discretion.
- It conferred on the **legislative assembly of Delhi** the power to make laws on all matters in the **state list** and the **concurrent list** except **public order, police and land**.
- It provided that the laws made by **Parliament** and by the **legislative assembly of Delhi** shall have effect **concurrently** subject to certain conditions.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Which Amendment of the Constitution provides that no law passed to give effect to Directive Principles of State Policy contained in Articles 39 (b) & (c) shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it abridges the rights conferred by Articles 14 and 19?
questions.questionText: Which Amendment of the Constitution provides that no law passed to give effect to Directive Principles of State Policy contained in Articles 39 (b) & (c) shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it abridges the rights conferred by Articles 14 and 19?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **25th Amendment** of the Constitution, enacted in **1971**, provides that no law passed to give effect to **Directive Principles of State Policy** contained in **Articles 39 (b) & (c)** shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it abridges the rights conferred by **Articles 14 and 19**.
- **Article 39 (b) & (c)** deal with the principles of securing **equitable distribution of material resources** and **preventing concentration of wealth** and means of production.
- **Article 14** guarantees **equality before law** and **equal protection of laws**.
- **Article 19** guarantees **six freedoms**, such as **freedom of speech and expression**, **freedom of assembly**, **freedom of association**, **freedom of movement**, **freedom of residence and settlement**, and **freedom of profession**.
**

Important Tips-The **25th Amendment** also added a new **clause (4)** to **Article 31**, which states that no law giving effect to the policy of securing the principles specified in **Article 39 (b) or (c)** shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not provide for compensation or that it provides for **inadequate compensation** for acquisition or requisition of property.
- **Article 31** deals with the **right to property**, which was later deleted from the list of **fundamental rights** by the **44th Amendment** in **1978**.
- The **25th Amendment** was enacted to give **primacy** to the **Directive Principles** over the **Fundamental Rights** in matters of **social and economic justice**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which one of the following is not included in the fundamental right to equality as enshrined in the Indian Constitution?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not included in the fundamental right to equality as enshrined in the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Economic equality** is **not mentioned** in any of the articles related to the **right to equality**.
**Economic equality** is more related to the **directive principles of state policy**, which are **not enforceable by courts** but are **guidelines** for the State to follow while making policies and laws.
- **Equality before law**: **Article 14** ensures that all individuals are **equal before the law** and are entitled to **equal protection of the laws**.
- **Social equality**: **Article 15** prohibits **discrimination** on the grounds of **religion, race, caste, sex**, or place of birth and promotes **social equality**.
- **Equal opportunity**: **Article 16** guarantees **equality of opportunity** in matters of **public employment**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: Which one of the following rights cannot be suspended or restricted even during National Emergency?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following rights cannot be suspended or restricted even during National Emergency?

options.a: Right to reside and settle in any part of the country.
options.b: Right to life and personal liberty.
options.c: Right to move freely throughout the territory of India.
options.d: Right to carry on any profession or business.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. A **national emergency** is a situation of crisis that threatens the **security, unity, and integrity** of the country.
It can be declared by the **President of India** under **Article 352** of the Constitution on the grounds of **war, external aggression, or armed rebellion**.
According to **Article 359**, when a **national emergency** is declared, the **President** can suspend the right to move any court for the enforcement of any of the **fundamental rights** except **Articles 20 and 21**.
**

Important Tips
- **Article 20**: **Protection in respect of conviction for offences**. It prohibits **ex post facto laws, double jeopardy, and self-incrimination**.
- **Article 21**: **Protection of life and personal liberty**. It states that no person shall be deprived of his **life or personal liberty** except according to **procedure established by law**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: Under which of the following articles the President of India can suspend the enforcement of Fundamental Rights (except Articles 20, 21)?
questions.questionText: Under which of the following articles the President of India can suspend the enforcement of Fundamental Rights (except Articles 20, 21)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 359** of the Constitution authorizes the **President of India** to suspend the right to move any court for the enforcement of **Fundamental Rights** during **National Emergency** except **Article 20 and Article 21**.
**

Important Tips
- **Article 358** deals with regulations that give rights to the state government to suspend the **Fundamental Rights** during **war or external aggression** but not **armed rebellion**.
- **Article 13** deals with the **laws inconsistent with or in derogation of fundamental rights**.
- Under **Article 356** of the Constitution of India, if a state government is unable to function according to **Constitutional provisions**, the **Union government** can take **direct control** of the state machinery.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: The 79th Amendment of the Indian Constitution is related to-
questions.questionText: The 79th Amendment of the Indian Constitution is related to-

options.d: Reservation of SCs and STs in the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **79th Amendment** of the Constitution of India, officially known as **The Constitution (Seventy-ninth Amendment) Act, 1999**, extended the period of **reservation of seats** for the **Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes** in the **Lok Sabha** and the **State Legislative Assemblies** for another ten years, i.e., up to **26 January 2010**.
This was done by amending **Article 334** of the Constitution, which provides for the **reservation of seats** for the **SCs and STs** and the **representation of the Anglo-Indian community by nomination** in the **Lok Sabha** and the **State Legislative Assemblies**.

questions.questionText: Match List-I with List-II and select the correct answer using the code given below the lists: List-I (Article) A. Fundamental Duties B. Parliament can amend Fundamental Rights C. Doctrine of Basic Structure of the Constitution D. Prohibition of Trafficking in Human Beings List-II (Provision) 1. Minerva Mills Case 2. Article 23 of the Constitution 3. 42nd Amendment 4. Keshvanand Bharti Case Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- The **42nd Amendment Act of 1976** added a new **Part IV A** to the Constitution which consists of **Article 51-A**, enlisting ten **fundamental duties** of the citizens.
- The **Minerva Mills Case of 1980** was a landmark judgment of the **Supreme Court** that upheld the **doctrine of basic structure** and struck down two clauses of the **42nd Amendment Act** that gave power to **Parliament** to amend any part of the Constitution, including the **fundamental rights**.
- The **Kesavananda Bharti Case of 1973** was a historic verdict of the **Supreme Court** that propounded the **doctrine of basic structure** for the first time.
- It held that **Parliament** has the power to amend any part of the Constitution, but it cannot alter or destroy the **basic or essential features** or framework of the Constitution.
- **Article 23** of the Constitution prohibits **trafficking in human beings** and other forms of **forced labour**, and declares them as **offences punishable by law**.
- It also empowers **Parliament** to enact laws to prevent such evils and provide for their **punishment**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Which one of the following Bills must be passed by each House of the Indian Parliament separately by the special majority?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following Bills must be passed by each House of the Indian Parliament separately by the special majority?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **Constitution Amendment Bill** must be passed by each House of the Indian Parliament separately by a **special majority**.
A **special majority** means that the bill must be approved by a majority of the **total membership of each House** and also by a majority of not less than **two-thirds of the members present and voting** in each House.
This **special majority** requirement ensures that any amendment to the Indian Constitution is subjected to a rigorous and **broad-based process** of consideration and approval.
**

Important Tips-An **Ordinary Bill** is concerned with any matter other than financial matters.
- It can be introduced in **either House of Parliament** by any member with a seven-day notice.
- It has to be passed by both Houses by a **simple majority** of members present and voting.
- A **Money Bill** is concerned with **financial matters** like taxation, expenditure, borrowing, etc. It can be introduced only in the **Lok Sabha** by a minister with the **prior permission of the President**.
- It has to be passed by the **Lok Sabha** by a **simple majority** of members present and voting.
- A **Financial Bill** is also concerned with financial matters. It has to be passed by both Houses by a **simple majority** of members present and voting.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Right to education to all children between the age group of 6 to 14 years is-
questions.questionText: Right to education to all children between the age group of 6 to 14 years is-

options.a: Included in the Directive Principles of State Policy

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Right to education** is included in the **right to life** enshrined in **Article 21** of the Constitution of India, which provides the right to **live with dignity**.
The **Supreme Court of India** has interpreted **Article 21** to include the **right to education** as a part of the **right to life and personal liberty**.
**Right to education** is also a **fundamental right** for children of age **6 to 14 years** under **Article 21A** of the Constitution of India, which was inserted by the **Constitution (Eighty-sixth Amendment) Act, 2002**. It provides that The state shall provide **free and compulsory education** to all children of age of **6 to 14 years** in such manner as the state may, by law, determine.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which one of the following rights is available under Article 19(l)read with Article 21:
questions.questionText: Which one of the following rights is available under Article 19(l)read with Article 21:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Article 19 (1) (d)** of the Constitution of India guarantees the right to **freedom of movement** to all citizens.
It means that every citizen has the right to **move freely** throughout the territory of India, without any unreasonable restriction or interference by the State.
This right is essential for the **development of personality**, **social and cultural interaction**, and **national integration**. **Article 21** of the Constitution of India guarantees the right to **life and personal liberty** to all persons. It means that no person shall be deprived of his **life or personal liberty** except according to the **procedure established by law**.
This right is the **most fundamental of all rights** and includes various other rights that are necessary for a **dignified human existence**.
The **Supreme Court of India** (**Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India, 1978**) has held that the **right to travel abroad** is a part of the right to **freedom of movement** under **Article 19 (1) (d)** as well as the **right to life and personal liberty** under **Article 21** of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: Which part of Constitution of India describes the model of Welfare State?
questions.questionText: Which part of Constitution of India describes the model of Welfare State?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Directive Principles of State Policy** aim to create **social and economic conditions** under which the citizens can lead a **good life**. They also aim to establish **social and economic democracy** through a **welfare state**.
The idea of **welfare states** arises from the **philosophy of compassion** towards the weaker sections. For example, the government provides food grains to people at a very **subsidised rate** under the **National Food Security Act, 2013**.
**

Important Tips Key features of **DPSPs** which describes the model of **Welfare State**:
- To Minimise **inequalities in income, status, facilities and opportunities** (**Art. 38**).
- To provide opportunities for **healthy development of children** (**Art. 38**).
- To ensure **equal pay for equal work** for men and women (**Art. 39A**).
- To secure the **right to work, to education and to public assistance** in cases of unemployment, old age, sickness and disablement (**Art. 41**).
- To make provision for **just and humane conditions of work** and **maternity relief** (**Art. 42**).
- To secure a **living wage**, a decent **standard of life** and **social and cultural opportunities** for all workers (**Art. 43**).
- To provide **early childhood care and education** for all children until they complete the age of six years (**Art. 45**).
- To promote the **educational and economic interests of SCs, STs, and other weaker sections** of the society and to protect them from **social injustice and exploitation** (**Art. 46**).
- To raise the **level of nutrition** and the **standard of living** of people and to improve **public health** (**Art. 47**).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: According to the Indian Constitution, the Constitutional amendment of the following topics requires consent of at least half of State legislatures-
1. Federal provision of the Constitution
2. Rights of the Supreme Court
3. The process of Constitutional Amendment
4. Formation of new States or transformation in the borders and names of the states Code:
questions.questionText: According to the Indian Constitution, the Constitutional amendment of the following topics requires consent of at least half of State legislatures- 1. Federal provision of the Constitution 2. Rights of the Supreme Court 3. The process of Constitutional Amendment 4. Formation of new States or transformation in the borders and names of the states Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The provisions of the Constitution which are related to the **federal structure** of the polity can be amended by a **special majority** of the **Parliament** and also with the **consent of half of the state legislatures** by a **simple majority**.
The following provisions can be amended in this way:
- 1. **Election of the President** and its manner.
- 2. Extent of the **executive power** of the Union and the states.
- 3. **Supreme Court and high courts**.
- 4. Distribution of **legislative powers** between the Union and the states.
- 5. Any of the lists in the **Seventh Schedule**.
- 6. **Representation of states in Parliament**.
- 7. Power of **Parliament** to amend the Constitution and its procedure (**Article 368 itself**).
**Statement 4 is incorrect:**Formation of new States**or transformation in the borders and names of the states can be amended by a**simple majority**of the two Houses of**Parliament outside the scope of Article 368**.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: In the event of the Upper House rejecting a Constitutional Amendment Bill passed by the Lower House-
questions.questionText: In the event of the Upper House rejecting a Constitutional Amendment Bill passed by the Lower House-

options.a: The Bill will have to be dropped
options.b: A joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament may be convened to consider and pass the Bill
options.c: The Bill shall be deemed to have been passed by both the Houses of the Parliament if the Lok Sabha passes it again by a two-third majority
options.d: The Bill shall be deemed to have been passed by both the Houses of the Parliament despite the disapproval of the Bill by the Upper House

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Bill will have to be dropped**. This is because a **Constitutional Amendment Bill** requires the approval of both Houses of **Parliament** by a **special majority** of not less than **two-thirds of the members present and voting**.
Therefore, if the **Upper House** rejects such a Bill, it **cannot be passed by a joint sitting** or by a re-passage by the **Lower House**. The Bill will have to be dropped or reintroduced with changes acceptable to both Houses.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following statements regarding Fundamental Duties is not true:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements regarding Fundamental Duties is not true:

options.a: They can be enforced by writs
options.b: They can be promoted only by constitutional methods
options.c: They can be used for interpreting ambiguous statutes
options.d: The performance of any duty comes within the sphere of constitutional law which court has to decid

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Fundamental duties** are **not enforceable by any court of law**. They are only the **moral obligations** of the citizens and there is **no legal sanction** for their violation or non-compliance.
Option b is correct: **They can be promoted only by constitutional methods**: This means that the citizens should respect the constitutional provisions and procedures while performing their duties and not resort to any **unlawful or violent means**.
Option c is correct: **They can be used for interpreting ambiguous statutes**: This means that the courts can use the **fundamental duties** as a guide or a tool to resolve any ambiguity or doubt in the meaning or scope of any law or statute.
Option d is correct: **The performance of any duty comes within the sphere of constitutional law which court has to decide**: This means that the courts have the power and authority to **adjudicate** any matter or dispute related to the performance or non-performance of any duty by the citizens or the state.
**

Important Tips Features of **Fundamental Duties** of India:
- Some of them are **moral duties** while others are **civic duties**.
- For instance, cherishing **noble ideals of freedom struggle** is a moral precept and respecting the **Constitution, National Flag and National Anthem** is a civic duty.
- They refer to such values which have been a part of the **Indian tradition, mythology, religions and practices**.
- In other words, they essentially contain just a **codification of tasks** integral to the Indian way of life.
- Unlike some of the **Fundamental Rights** which extend to all persons whether citizens or foreigners, the **Fundamental Duties** are **confined to citizens only** and do not extend to foreigners-Like the **Directive Principles**, the **fundamental duties** are also **non-justiciable**.
- The Constitution **does not provide for their direct enforcement** by the courts.
- Moreover, there is **no legal sanction** against their violation. However, **Parliament** is free to enforce them with suitable legislation.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following rights are not available to all persons in India?
1. Equality Before the Law
2. Right Against Discrimination
3. Freedom to Move Freely throughout the Country
4. Right to Contest Election Select your answer by using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following rights are not available to all persons in India? 1. Equality Before the Law 2. Right Against Discrimination 3. Freedom to Move Freely throughout the Country 4. Right to Contest Election Select your answer by using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The rights that are **not available to all persons** in India are:
- **Right against discrimination** (**Article 15**)
- This right prohibits discrimination on grounds of **religion, race, caste, sex** or place of birth.
- It is available **only to the citizens of India**.
- **Freedom to move freely throughout the country** (**Article 19 (1) (d)**)
- This right allows citizens to **move freely** throughout the territory of India.
- It is one of the **six freedoms** guaranteed under **Article 19**, which are available **only to the citizens of India**.
- **Right to contest election**
- This right is **not explicitly mentioned** in the Constitution, but it is implied from **Article 84 and Article 173**, which prescribe the qualifications for being a member of **Parliament** and **State Legislature** respectively.
- One of the qualifications is that the person must be a **citizen of India**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: Identify the items included in the Directive Principles of State Policy:
1. Prohibition of Consumption of Liquor
2. Prohibition of employment of children in factories or mines
3. Prohibition of beggar or forced labour
4. Prohibition of untouchability
Select the correct answer with the help of the code given below:
questions.questionText: Identify the items included in the Directive Principles of State Policy: 1. Prohibition of Consumption of Liquor 2. Prohibition of employment of children in factories or mines 3. Prohibition of beggar or forced labour 4. Prohibition of untouchability Select the correct answer with the help of the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Option 1 is correct: According to **Article 47** of the **DPSP**, The State shall also endeavour to bring about **prohibition of the consumption except for medicinal purposes of intoxicating drinks and of drugs which are injurious to health**.
Option 2 is incorrect: **Prohibition of employment of children in factories or mines** is a **Fundamental Right** (**Not DPSP**) under **article 24**. **Article 24** says that No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other hazardous employment.
Option 3 is incorrect: **Prohibition of beggar or forced labour** is a **Fundamental Right** (**Not DPSP**) under **article 23**. **Article 23** prohibits the **trafficking in human beings, begar**, and any similar form of forced labor.
It also states that any contravention of this provision is **punishable by the law**. Option 4 is incorrect: **Prohibition of untouchability** is a **Fundamental Right** (**Not DPSP**) under **article 17**. It reads that **Untouchability** is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden. The enforcement of any disability arising out of Untouchability shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: In which of the following cases the Supreme Court limited the power of Parliament to amend the Constitution for the first time?
questions.questionText: In which of the following cases the Supreme Court limited the power of Parliament to amend the Constitution for the first time?

options.a: A.K. Gopalan Vs. The State of Madras
options.b: Golaknath Vs. The State of Punjab
options.c: Keshavananda Bharati Vs. The State of Kerala
options.d: Minerva Mills Vs. The Union of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Golaknath Vs. The State of Punjab** case was decided in **1967** and it was the **first time** that the **Supreme Court** limited the power of **Parliament** to amend the Constitution.
The court held that the **Parliament cannot amend** the fundamental rights enshrined in **Part III** of the Constitution and that any amendment made in contravention of this principle would be **void**.
**

Important Tips
- **Golaknath Vs. The State of Punjab** case overruled the earlier decisions of **A.K. Gopalan Vs. The State of Madras (**1950**)** and **Sajjan Singh Vs. State of Rajasthan (**1965**)**, which upheld the unlimited amending power of **Parliament**.
- However, the **Golaknath case** was itself overruled by **Keshavananda Bharati Vs. The State of Kerala (**1973**)**, which introduced the **doctrine of basic structure**, which limits the **Parliaments** amending power to preserve the essential features of the Constitution.
- The **Minerva Mills Vs. The Union of India (**1980**)** case applied the basic structure doctrine and struck down some provisions of the **42nd Amendment Act, 1976**, which sought to curtail the **judicial review** of constitutional amendments

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following provisions under the Constitution of India enabled the Supreme Court of India to deduce a fundamental right to equal pay for equal work?
questions.questionText: Which of the following provisions under the Constitution of India enabled the Supreme Court of India to deduce a fundamental right to equal pay for equal work?

options.a: The word 'socialist' used in the Preamble to the Constitution
options.b: (a) read with Article 14 of the Constitution
options.c: (a) read with Article 16 of the Constitution

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Supreme Court of India** has deduced a **fundamental right to equal pay for equal work** from the word **socialist** used in the **Preamble** to the Constitution as well as from **Article 14, 15 and 16** of the Constitution.
**Equal pay for equal work** is **not a constitutional right** or a fundamental right. It can be described through the interpretations of **Article 14, 15 and 16** which guarantee fundamental rights of **equality before law**, **protection against any kind of discrimination** and **equal opportunities** in the matters of public employment.
The word **socialist** used in the **Preamble** to the Constitution also enabled the **Supreme Court of India** to deduce a **fundamental right to equal pay for equal work**.
The **Preamble** declares India to be a **sovereign, socialist, secular, democratic republic**. The term **socialist** implies **social and economic justice** for all citizens.
It also implies that the State shall strive to **reduce inequalities in income, status, facilities and opportunities** among individuals and groups

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Consider the following statements about the Fundamental Rights in India:
1. They are a guarantee against state action.
2. They are enumerated in Part III of the Constitution.
3. They ensure social, economic and political justice.
4. They are unlike Bill of Rights in the U.S.A.
Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements about the Fundamental Rights in India: 1. They are a guarantee against state action. 2. They are enumerated in Part III of the Constitution. 3. They ensure social, economic and political justice. 4. They are unlike Bill of Rights in the U.S.A. Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct:**Fundamental Rights**are enshrined in**Part III**of the Constitution of India and are**enforceable by the courts**, subject to certain restrictions.** They protect the citizens from **arbitrary or discriminatory state action** that violates or abridges their rights.
**Statement 2 is correct:**Fundamental Rights**are listed in**Articles 12 to 35**of**Part III**of the Constitution of India.** There are six **Fundamental Rights**, namely, **Right to Equality, Right to Freedom, right against Exploitation, Right to Freedom of Religion, Cultural and Educational Rights, and Right to Constitutional Remedies**.
**Statement 3 is incorrect:**Fundamental Rights**are more concerned with**individual rights and liberties**that protect the citizens from arbitrary state action or discrimination.** They **do not directly ensure social, economic and political justice**, which is more of a goal of the **Directive Principles of State Policy** (**Part IV** of the Constitution).
**Statement 4 is incorrect:**Fundamental Rights**in India are**heavily inspired by the Bill of Rights in the U.S.A.**, which is the first ten amendments to the U.S. Constitution that guarantee civil liberties to the people.** The **Fundamental Rights** in India have their origins in many sources, including Englands **Bill of Rights**, the United States **Bill of Rights** and Frances **Declaration of the Rights of Man**.
**

Important Tips Features of **Fundamental Rights** in India:
- They are **universal**, which means they apply to all citizens of India, irrespective of their race, place of birth, religion, caste, creed, color, or sex.
- They are **justiciable**, which means they can be enforced by the courts, subject to certain restrictions.
- The **Supreme Court** and the **High Courts** can issue **writs** for the protection of **Fundamental Rights** under **Article 32 and Article 226** respectively.
- They are **not absolute**, which means they can be restricted or suspended by the **Parliament** or the **President** under certain circumstances, such as during a **national emergency**, martial law, or public interest.
- They are **amendable**, which means they can be changed or repealed by a constitutional amendment, if it **does not alter the basic structure** of the Constitution.
- They are **dynamic**, which means they can be interpreted and expanded by the **judiciary** according to the changing needs and aspirations of society.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following has been recognized as a fundamental right by the Supreme Court of India?
1. Right to Shelter
2. Right to Travel Abroad
3. Right to Equal Pay for Equal Work
Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following has been recognized as a fundamental right by the Supreme Court of India? 1. Right to Shelter 2. Right to Travel Abroad 3. Right to Equal Pay for Equal Work Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **Supreme Court of India** has recognized all the three options as **fundamental rights** of the citizens under the Constitution of India.
These are:
- **Right to Shelter**: The **Supreme Court** has held that the **right to shelter** is a part of the **right to life and personal liberty** under **Article 21** of the Constitution.
- It means that every citizen has the right to **adequate housing** that ensures human dignity, privacy, and security.
- The **Supreme Court** has also held that the State has a **duty to provide shelter** to the poor, homeless, and marginalized sections of society.
- **Right to Travel Abroad**: The **Supreme Court** has held that the **right to travel abroad** is a part of the right to **freedom of movement** under **Article 19 (1) (d)** of the Constitution.
- It means that every citizen has the right to go outside India and return to India without any unreasonable restriction or interference by the State.
- The **Supreme Court** has also held that the State can impose **reasonable restrictions** on this right in the interests of **sovereignty, integrity, and security** of India, friendly relations with foreign states, **public order**, or **morality** under **Article 19 (5)** of the Constitution.
- **Right to Equal Pay for Equal Work**: The **Supreme Court** has held that the **right to equal pay for equal work** is a part of the **right to equality** under **Article 14 and 16** of the Constitution.
- It means that every citizen has the right to receive **equal remuneration** for work of equal value without any discrimination on the grounds of sex, caste, religion, etc.
- The **Supreme Court** has also held that this right is a **constitutional goal** that is to be attained by the State in accordance with **Article 39 (d)** of the Constitution, which is a **directive principle of state policy**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: Which of the following was not added to the Directive Principles by the 42nd Amendment of the Constitution?
questions.questionText: Which of the following was not added to the Directive Principles by the 42nd Amendment of the Constitution?

options.a: Protection of Children and youth from exploitation
options.b: Equal justice and free legal aid
options.d: Participation of workers in the management of industries

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Uniform civil code** is given in the original constitution under **Article 44**. It states that The State shall endeavour to secure the citizen a **Uniform Civil Code** throughout the territory of India.
**

Important Tips Some **Directive Principles** are added in the original Constitution as per the needs arise with the time.
- The **42nd Amendment Act of 1976** added four new **Directive Principles** to the original list.
- They require the State:
- To secure opportunities for **healthy development of children** (**Article 39**).
- To promote **equal justice and to provide free legal aid** to the poor (**Article 39 A**).
- To take steps to secure the **participation of workers in the management of industries** (**Article 43 A**).
- To protect and improve the environment and to safeguard **forests and wild life** (**Article 48 A**).
- The **44th Amendment Act of 1978** added one more **Directive Principle**, which requires the State to minimise **inequalities in income, status, facilities and opportunities** (**Article 38**).
- The **86th Amendment Act of 2002** changed the subject-matter of **Article 45** and made **elementary education** a fundamental right under **Article 21 A**. The amended directive requires the State to provide **early childhood care and education** for all children until they complete the age of six years.
- The **97th Amendment Act of 2011** added a new **Directive Principle** relating to **cooperative societies**.
- It requires the state to promote **voluntary formation, autonomous functioning, democratic control and professional management** of co-operative societies (**Article 43B**).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which one of the following writs is regarded as the greatest safeguard for the liberty of a person?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following writs is regarded as the greatest safeguard for the liberty of a person?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The writ of **Habeas Corpus** is regarded as the **greatest safeguard for the liberty of a person**.
**Habeas Corpus** is a Latin term that means **to have the body.** It is a **legal action** through which a person can challenge the **lawfulness of their detention or imprisonment**.
The writ is used to seek the **release of a person** who is unlawfully detained or held against their will. The purpose of the writ of **Habeas Corpus** is to protect **individual liberty** and ensure that a person is not unlawfully deprived of their freedom.
It is considered a **fundamental safeguard** against arbitrary or illegal detention, providing a mechanism for individuals to challenge the lawfulness of their detention before a court of law.
**

Important Tips
- **Mandamus**: This writ means **we command**. It is used to order a **public official**, a public body, a corporation, an inferior court, a tribunal or a government to perform a duty that they are obliged to perform but have failed or refused to do so.
- **Prohibition**: This writ means **to forbid**. It is used to stop an inferior court, a tribunal or an authority from **exceeding its jurisdiction** or acting contrary to the rules of **natural justice**.
- It prevents them from continuing a proceeding that is **ultra vires** or invalid.
- **Certiorari**: This writ means **to be certified**. It is used to **transfer a case** pending before an inferior court, a tribunal or an authority to a **higher court** for review.
- It can also be used to **quash or nullify** an order already passed by an inferior court, a tribunal or an authority that is **ultra vires, unconstitutional, illegal, erroneous or without jurisdiction**.
- **Quo Warranto**: This writ means **by what authority**. It is used to challenge the **legality** of a persons claim to hold a **public office**.
- It prevents **usurpation of public office** by an ineligible or unqualified person.
- It can also be used to **oust** such person from the office if he or she is already holding it

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.a: K.M. Munshi was one of the members of the drafting committee of the Constitution.
options.b: The Constitution of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly.
options.c: The Panchayati Raj was recommended by Balwant Rai Mehta Committee Report-1957.
options.d: The President of India is the Guardian of Fundamental Rights under the Constitution.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **President of India** is **not the Guardian of Fundamental Rights** under the Constitution.
The **Supreme Court and High Courts** are the **guardians of Fundamental Rights** as they have the power to issue **writs** for their enforcement.
The **President of India**, on the other hand, is the head of the **executive branch** of the government and performs various ceremonial, constitutional, and diplomatic functions.
While the **President** has a significant role in upholding and defending the Constitution, the **direct responsibility** for the protection and enforcement of fundamental rights lies with the **judiciary**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Given below are two statements, one labelled as Assertion (A) and the other as Reason (R):
Assertion (A): MGNREGA is providing employment to at least one member of the eligible household for a minimum period of 100 days in a year.
Reason (R): Right to employment is enumerated in Part III of the Constitution.
Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.questionText: Given below are two statements, one labelled as Assertion (A) and the other as Reason (R): Assertion (A): MGNREGA is providing employment to at least one member of the eligible household for a minimum period of 100 days in a year. Reason (R): Right to employment is enumerated in Part III of the Constitution. Select the correct answer from the code given below:

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not a correct explanation of (A).

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **MGNREGA** is providing employment to at least one member of the eligible household for a **minimum period of 100 days** in a year.
Hence, **Assertion (A) is correct**. Whereas, **Right to employment** is **not enumerated** in **Part III** of the Constitution. It is given in **Part IV** of the Constitution.
Hence, correct answer is: **(A) is true, but (R) is false**. The **Part III** of the Constitution deal with **Fundamental Rights** (**Article 12-35**), while **Part IV** of the Constitution of India contains the **DPSP** (**Article 36-51**).
**Article 41** of the Indian Constitution directs the state to secure the **right to work, education and public assistance** in certain cases such as unemployment, old age, sickness and disablement.
**

Important Tips **Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA)**:
- The Indian Parliament passed **NREGA** in August **2005**. It came into force on February 2, **2006**. On 2nd October **2009**, the act **NREGA** was renamed **MGNREGA**.
- It is a **poverty alleviation programme** of the Government of India, which provides the **legal Right to Work**.
- It aims to enhance **livelihood security** in rural areas by providing at least **100 days** of guaranteed wage employment in a financial year to every household whose adult members volunteer to do unskilled manual work.
- **Funding**:
- It is shared between the Centre and the States.
- The **Central Government** bears **100 per cent** of the cost of unskilled labour, **75 percent** of the cost of semi-skilled and skilled labour, **75 percent** of the cost of materials and **6 percent** of the administrative costs.
- **Time-Bound Guarantee of Work** as employment must be provided within **15 days** of being demanded.
- In case of failure, an **unemployment allowance** must be given.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: The Fundamental Duties of a citizen do not include duty -
questions.questionText: The Fundamental Duties of a citizen do not include duty -

options.a: To protect and improve the natural environment
options.b: To cherish and follow the noble ideals which inspired our national struggle for freedom
options.c: To strive towards abolition of untouchability
options.d: To develop scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of enquiry and reform

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **fundamental duties** list is given in **Article 51A** of the Indian Constitution. These are the **moral obligations** of every citizen of India to promote the spirit of **patriotism** and preserve **Indias unity**.
The duty **To strive towards the abolition of untouchability** is **not included** in the **Fundamental Duties** of a citizen in India. The other options, (a) **To protect and improve the natural environment**, (b) **To cherish and follow the noble ideals which inspired our national struggle for freedom**, and (d) **To develop scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of enquiry and reform**, are included as **Fundamental Duties** of a citizen in the Indian Constitution.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: Fundamental Rights-
questions.questionText: Fundamental Rights-

options.b: Can be suspended by order of Prime Minister
options.c: Can be suspended on the will of President

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Fundamental rights** of citizens of India can be **suspended during a state of emergency** declared by the **President of India** on the recommendation of the **Union Cabinet** under **Article 352** of the Constitution. During a state of emergency:
- All the freedoms guaranteed in **Article 19** of the Constitution shall be **automatically suspended**.
- There is no need for a separate order to suspend them.
- The **suspension of the right to move to any court** for the enforcement of fundamental rights can only be done by the **order of the President of India** under **Article 359** of the Constitution.
- The **President** in his order will mention the right whose enforcement will be suspended.
- The order of the **President** may extend to the whole or any part of the territory of India.
- The suspension of fundamental rights will exist as long as the **emergency period**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: What is meant by 'Rule of Law'?
questions.questionText: What is meant by 'Rule of Law'?

options.a: One act for all and one judiciary for all
options.b: One act for all and one State for all
options.c: One State for all and one judiciary for all
options.d: All acts for one and one judiciary for all
options.e: None of the above/ More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **rule of law** is a political and legal principle that means all people and institutions are subject to and accountable to law that is **fairly applied and enforced**.
It also implies that **no one is above the law**, that the government is **not arbitrary**, and that the laws are **consistent and impartial**.
In India, the **rule of law** is enshrined in the Constitution, which prescribes the ideas of **justice, liberty and equality** for all citizens.
The Constitution also provides for **separation of powers**, **judicial review**, **fundamental rights**, and **directive principles of state policy** to ensure that the **rule of law** is upheld in the country.
The most appropriate answer is
- **One act for all and one judiciary for all**.
This option reflects the idea that everyone is subject to the same laws and the same judicial system, regardless of their status or power.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: Which of the following is/are listed among the Directive Principles in Part-IV of the Constitution of India?
1. Equal Pay for Equal Work
2. Uniform Civil Code
3. Small family norm
4. Education through mother tongue at primary level Code:
questions.questionText: Which of the following is/are listed among the Directive Principles in Part-IV of the Constitution of India? 1. Equal Pay for Equal Work 2. Uniform Civil Code 3. Small family norm 4. Education through mother tongue at primary level Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Option I is correct: In **Article 39** under **DPSP**, it is given that there shall be **equal pay for equal work for both men and women**.
Option II is correct: In **Article 44** under **DPSP**, reference related to **Uniform civil code** is given in the constitution. **Article 44** states that The State shall endeavour to secure the citizen a **Uniform Civil Code** throughout the territory of India.
Option III is incorrect: **Small family norm** is **not given** in the Constitution of India explicitly. Option IV is incorrect: **Education through mother tongue at primary level** is **not given** in the **DPSP** in the Constitution.
**Article 350 A** describes that It shall be the endeavor of every State and of every local authority within the State to provide adequate facilities for instruction in the **mother tongue** at the primary stage of education to children belonging to linguistic minority groups.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: The Supreme Court has held that hoisting the National Flag atop the private buildings is a fundamental right of every citizen under:
questions.questionText: The Supreme Court has held that hoisting the National Flag atop the private buildings is a fundamental right of every citizen under:

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Hoisting the National Flag** atop the private buildings is a **fundamental right** of every citizen under **Article 19 (1) (a)** of the Constitution, which guarantees the right to **freedom of speech and expression**.
This right includes the right to express ones views and opinions through any mode of communication, such as words, signs, symbols, gestures, pictures, etc. It also includes the right to **receive information** from any source and **disseminate** it to others.
However, **hoisting the National Flag** is **not explicitly mentioned** or specially provided in **Article 19 (1) (a)** of the Constitution. It is **implied or derived** from the wider freedom of expression guaranteed by this article. The **Supreme Court** has interpreted this article to include the **freedom of press** as an integral part of the freedom of speech and expression.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following rights conferred by the Constitution of India is also available to non-citizens?
questions.questionText: Which of the following rights conferred by the Constitution of India is also available to non-citizens?

options.c: Freedom to Move and Settle in any Part of the Country

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The right under **Article 32** is available to **anyone whose rights are being violated, not just citizens**
- the distinction is important because, for instance, while the right to **freedom of speech** in **Article 19(l)(a)** is provided to citizens, the **right to life and personal liberty** is guaranteed to **all persons** in India.
The right of a **foreign national** (i.e. a natural person) to file a **writ petition** to enforce the **fundamental rights** guaranteed to him by the Constitution of India, was upheld by the apex Court in the case of **Louis De Raedt vs. Union of India** wherein, a petition was filed to enforce **Article 21** of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: According to which Article National Emergency may be declared by the president of india?
questions.questionText: According to which Article National Emergency may be declared by the president of india?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Under **Article 352**, the **president** can declare a **national emergency** when the security of India or a part of it is threatened by **war or external aggression or armed rebellion**.
The **President** can declare a **national emergency** even before the actual occurrence of war or armed rebellion or external aggression. **

Important Tips
- **Article 370** of the Indian constitution is an article that granted **special autonomous status** to the state of **Jammu and Kashmir**.
- On 5 August **2019**, the government of India revoked the special constitutional status of the erstwhile state of **Jammu and Kashmir** under **Article 370** of the Constitution,
- Under **Article 371**, the **President** is authorised to provide that the **Governor of Maharashtra and that of Gujarat** would have **special responsibility** for: The establishment of separate development boards for **Vidarbha, Marathwada** and the rest of Maharashtra, **Saurashtra, Kutch** and the rest of Gujarat.
- **Article 395** repeals the **Indian Independence Act, 1947**, and the **Government of India Act, 1935**, but not including the **Abolition of Privy Council Jurisdiction Act, 1949**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following is given the power to enforce the Fundamental Rights by the Constitution?
questions.questionText: Which of the following is given the power to enforce the Fundamental Rights by the Constitution?

options.d: The Supreme Court and High Courts

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. According to the Constitution of India, the **Supreme Court and High Courts** are given the power to **enforce the Fundamental Rights** by the Constitution.
**Article 32** of the Constitution grants the **right to remedies** for the enforcement of the **fundamental rights** of a citizen of India who has suffered.
This makes the **fundamental rights enforceable in the court of law**. The aggrieved party can directly move to the **Supreme Court** or the **High Court** for the protection of their rights.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Which of the following Constitutional Amendment Acts was termed as Mini Constitution?
questions.questionText: Which of the following Constitutional Amendment Acts was termed as Mini Constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **42nd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1976** is often referred to as the **Mini Constitution** because of its extensive scope and significant changes to various parts of the Indian Constitution.
It was passed during the period of the **Emergency** and introduced numerous amendments that aimed to strengthen the position of the government and **centralize power**.
**

Important Tips Features of **42nd Constitutional Amendment Act**:
- It added the words **socialist, secular and integrity** to the **Preamble** of the Constitution.
- It laid down the **Fundamental Duties** of Indian citizens in a new **Part IV-A** of the Constitution.
- It extended the term of the **Lok Sabha** and the **state legislative assemblies** from **five to six years**.
- It transferred **five subjects** from the **state list** to the **concurrent list**, namely **education, forests, weights and measures, protection of wild animals and birds, and administration of justice**.
- It curtailed the power of the **Supreme Court and the High Courts** to review the **constitutional validity** of laws and gave more power to the **Parliament** to amend the Constitution.
- It changed the relationship between the Centre and the states by giving more authority to the Centre in matters of **deployment of armed forces**, property taxes, elections, inter-state trade and commerce, etc.
- It gave more power to the **Prime Ministers Office** and reduced the role of the **President** and the **Governors** to mere formal heads

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which Article of the Constitution gives precedence to Constitutional provisions over the laws made by the Union Parliament/ State Legislatures?
questions.questionText: Which Article of the Constitution gives precedence to Constitutional provisions over the laws made by the Union Parliament/ State Legislatures?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Article 13** of the Constitution of India describes the means for **judicial review**. It states that all laws in force in the territory of India immediately before the commencement of this Constitution, in so far as they are **inconsistent** with the provisions of this Part (**Part III**), shall, to the extent of such inconsistency, be **void**.
It also states that the State shall not make any law which **takes away or abridges** the rights conferred by this Part and any law made in contravention of this clause shall, to the extent of the contravention, be **void**.
It also defines **law** and **laws in force** for the purpose of this article. It also provides that nothing in this article shall apply to any amendment of this Constitution made under **Article 368**.
**Article 13** of the Constitution gives **precedence** to constitutional provisions over the laws made by the **Union Parliament/State Legislatures** because it declares that any law that is **inconsistent** with or derogates from the **fundamental rights** (**Part III**) shall be null and void.
It also empowers the courts to **strike down** any such law that violates or infringes the **fundamental rights**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: Mainly on whose advice is the President's Rule imposed in the State?
questions.questionText: Mainly on whose advice is the President's Rule imposed in the State?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **President of India** imposes **Presidents rule** in any state on the **recommendation of the Governor** of the concerned state.
**Presidents rule** means the **suspension of state government** and the **direct rule of the center**. Presidential rule or **state emergency** can be imposed in any state under **Art 356** of the constitution of India.
**

Important Tips-The proclamation of the **presidential rule** shall be approved by the **Parliament** within **two months** from the date of its issue by a **simple majority**.
- After the proclamation of Presidential rule, **state assembly get dissolved**.
- This article was used for the first time in the state of **Punjab**.

statementLines: Which of the following statement is correct regarding Article 368 of the Constitution of India?
(i) Article 368 was amended by the 24th Constitution Amendment Act, 1971.
(ii) Article 368 was amended by the 101 st Constitution Amendment Act, 2016.
Codes:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statement is correct regarding Article 368 of the Constitution of India? (i) Article 368 was amended by the 24th Constitution Amendment Act, 1971. (ii) Article 368 was amended by the 101 st Constitution Amendment Act, 2016. Codes:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement I is correct:**Article 368**was amended by the**24th Constitution Amendment Act, 1971**to affirm the power of**Parliament**to amend any part of the Constitution and to make it mandatory for the**President**to give assent to any**Constitutional Amendment Bill**.**

**Statement II is correct:**Article 368**was also amended by the**101st Constitution Amendment Act, 2016**to introduce a new**clause (2A)**that provides for**ratification by at least half of the State Legislatures**for amendments related to**Goods and Services Tax (GST)**.**

questions.questionText: When was the special rights and privy purse enjoyed by the Indian Princely States abolished?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **special rights and privy purse** enjoyed by the **Indian Princely States** were abolished in **1971** by the **26th Amendment** of the Indian Constitution.
**

Important Tips-The **privy purse** was a specific amount of money that was paid annually by the Indian government to the **rulers of princely states** and their successors who had acceded to India in **1947** and **1949**.
- The amount of **privy purse** was determined by many factors like **states revenue**, states status under British rule, etc.
