statementLines: Article 24 of the Constitution of India prohibits the employment of child in factories related to hazardous works.
Such prohibition is
questions.questionText: Article 24 of the Constitution of India prohibits the employment of child in factories related to hazardous works. Such prohibition is
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Article 24** of the Constitution of India is a **fundamental right** that prohibits the **employment of children below the age of fourteen years** in any factory, mine, or any other **hazardous occupation**.
The article aims to protect the **health and education** of children and to prevent their **exploitation**. The prohibition of **child labour** under **Article 24** is **absolute and unconditional**.
It means that no child below the age of fourteen years can be employed in any factory, mine, or any other hazardous occupation, irrespective of the **nature, duration, or condition of work**.
The **Supreme Court of India** has upheld the **absolute prohibition** of child labour under **Article 24** and has issued various directions and guidelines to ensure its effective implementation.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: The scope of the Article 21 of the Constitution was expanded to include Right to Education, because of the decision of Supreme Court in the case
questions.questionText: The scope of the Article 21 of the Constitution was expanded to include Right to Education, because of the decision of Supreme Court in the case

options.b: Govind v / s State of Madhya Pradesh
options.c: Parmanand Katara v/s Union of India
options.d: Chameli Singh v/ s State of Uttar Pradesh

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Supreme Court of India** has expanded the scope of **Article 21** to include the **right to education** as a part of the **right to life and personal liberty**.
The landmark case that initiated this expansion was **Unnikrishnan v/s Andhra Pradesh (**1993**)**. In this case, the **Supreme Court** held that the **right to education** is **implicit in the right to life**, as education is essential for the development of **human personality and potential**.
The **Supreme Court** also held that the **right to education** is a **fundamental right** for children up to the age of 14 years, as it is enshrined in **Article 45** of the Constitution, which is a **directive principle of state policy**.
**

Important Tips
- **Govind v/s State of Madhya Pradesh (**1975**)** is a case that expanded the scope of **Article 21** to include the **right to privacy**.
- **Parmanand Katara v/s Union of India (**1989**)** is a case that expanded the scope of **Article 21** to include the **right to medical aid**.
- **Chameli Singh v/s State of Uttar Pradesh (**1996**)** is a case that expanded the scope of **Article 21** to include the **right to shelter**.

questions.questionText: When were the Fundamental Duties mentioned in the Constitution?

options.a: At the time of framing of Constitution

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **Fundamental Duties** of citizens were added to the Constitution by the **42nd Amendment in 1976**, upon the recommendations of the **Swaran Singh Committee** that was constituted by the government earlier that year.
**Fundamental Duties** help to regulate the **behaviour** of the citizens and to bring about **excellence** in all the spheres of the citizens.
The **42nd Amendment** also made various other changes to the Constitution, such as adding the terms **socialist, secular and integrity** to the **Preamble**, curtailing the power of the **Supreme Court and High Courts**, and giving more power to the **Prime Minister**

Therefore, the government (**Central, state and local**) cannot be compelled to implement them.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: By which of the following case Parliament got the right to amend Fundamental Rights?
questions.questionText: By which of the following case Parliament got the right to amend Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The case that gave **Parliament** the right to amend fundamental rights was the **Kesavananda Bharati Case of 1973**. In this landmark judgment, the **Supreme Court** propounded the **doctrine of basic structure**, which holds that there is a **basic structure** or essential features of the Constitution that **cannot be altered or destroyed** by any amendment.
The court held that **Parliament** has the power to amend any part of the Constitution, including the **fundamental rights**, but it **cannot violate the basic structure**.
The court overruled the previous decision of **Golaknath Case of 1967**, which had held that **Parliament** cannot amend the fundamental rights at all.

statementLines: Fundamental Duties were incorporated in the Constitution on the recommendation of
questions.questionText: Fundamental Duties were incorporated in the Constitution on the recommendation of
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Swaran Singh Committee** was set up by the Congress government in **1976** to make recommendations about **fundamental duties**, the need and necessity of which was felt during the operation of the **internal emergency (**1975**-**1977**)**.
The committee recommended the inclusion of a chapter on **fundamental duties** in the Constitution. It stressed that the citizens should become conscious that in addition to the enjoyment of **rights**, they also have certain **duties** to perform as well.
**

Important Tips
- **Shah Commission**: The **Shah Commission**, officially known as the **Commission of Inquiry into the Affairs of the Indian National Congress**, was established in **1977** to investigate allegations of **corruption and malpractices** by the government during the period of the state of emergency in India from **1975** to **1977**.
- **Administrative Reforms Commission**: The **Administrative Reforms Commission (ARC)** is a committee set up by the government of India to recommend measures to improve the **efficiency and effectiveness** of the public administration system.
- **Santhanam Committee**: The **Santhanam Committee**, officially known as the **Committee on Prevention of Corruption**, was appointed in **1962** to investigate and suggest measures to **combat corruption** in India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Select the correct chronological order of the following judgements dealing with Fundamental Rights:
questions.questionText: Select the correct chronological order of the following judgements dealing with Fundamental Rights:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The correct chronological order of the judgements dealing with **Fundamental Rights** is:
- **(D) A.K Gopalan v/s State of Madras (**1950**)**
- This case dealt with the validity of **preventive detention** and the scope of **Article 21** of the Constitution.
- The **Supreme Court** held that the **Fundamental Rights** are **not absolute** and can be **restricted by law**.
- It also held that each **Fundamental Right** is **separate and distinct**, and there is **no inter-relationship** between them.
- **(A) Golaknath v/s State of Punjab (**1967**)**
- This case dealt with the power of **Parliament** to amend the **Fundamental Rights**.
- The **Supreme Court** overruled its previous decisions and held that **Parliament cannot amend** the **Fundamental Rights**, as they are **sacrosanct and transcendental**.
- It also held that **Article 368** only lays down the **procedure for amendment**, and any amendment is a **law under Article 13**, which **cannot abridge or take away** the **Fundamental Rights**.
- **(B) Keshvananda Bharati v/s State of Kerala (**1973**)**
- This case dealt with the validity of the **24th, 25th and 29th Amendment Acts**, which curtailed the **Fundamental Rights** relating to **property** and introduced the **doctrine of basic structure**.
- The **Supreme Court** overruled the **Golaknath case** and upheld the validity of these amendments.
- It held that **Parliament** has the power to amend any part of the Constitution, including the **Fundamental Rights**, but it **cannot alter or destroy the basic structure** or essential features of the Constitution.
- **(C) Minerva Mills v/s Union of India (**1980**)**
- This case dealt with the validity of the **42nd Amendment Act**, which inserted two clauses in **Article 368**, giving power to **Parliament** to amend any part of the Constitution, including the **basic structure**, and making such amendments **immune from judicial review**.
- The **Supreme Court** struck down these clauses as **unconstitutional** and violative of the **basic structure**.
- It reaffirmed the **doctrine of basic structure** and held that there is a **harmony and balance** between the **Fundamental Rights** and the **Directive Principles**, which constitute the **core of the basic structure**.

Resolution can be passed by Assembly. 2. Resolution can be rejected by Assembly. 3. No Amendment shall be proposed to such a resolution Select the correct answer using code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Article 368 (2)** of the Constitution of India states that some amendments also require the **ratification by not less than half of the states** by resolution passed by their legislatures before they are presented to the President for assent.
If a bill comes to **Rajasthan Legislative Assembly** after passing by **Parliament of India** for resolution to ratify such an amendment, then:
- **Resolution can be passed by Assembly.** This is true, as the Assembly has the power to **ratify** the amendment by a **simple majority** of members present and voting.
- **Resolution can be rejected by Assembly.** This is true, as the Assembly has the power to **reject** the amendment by a **simple majority** of members present and voting.
- **No Amendment shall be proposed to such a resolution.** This is true, as the Assembly **cannot propose any amendment** to the bill passed by Parliament.

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding Article 356 of the Constitution:

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding Article 356 of the Constitution: (i) Failure of Constitutional Machinery in States is an objective reality. (ii) A Proclamation under this Article can be reviewed by the Supreme Court. (iii) Along with this Proclamation, the State Legislative Assembly can be dissolved. (iv) This Proclamation has to be approved by each House of Parliament within two months. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. According to **Article 356**, **Presidents Rule** can be imposed on any state of India on the grounds of the **failure of the constitutional machinery**.
The proclamation of **Presidents Rule** should be approved in both Houses of **Parliament** within **two months** of its issue. The approval is through a **simple majority**. A Proclamation under this Article can be **reviewed by the Supreme Court**.
Along with this Proclamation, the **State Legislative Assembly cannot be dissolved**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: The President's power to veto a Bill for amendment of the Constitution has been taken away by substituting the word 'shall give his assent' by which amendment?
questions.questionText: The President's power to veto a Bill for amendment of the Constitution has been taken away by substituting the word 'shall give his assent' by which amendment?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **Presidents power to veto** a Bill for the amendment of the Constitution was taken away by the **24th Amendment** to the Indian Constitution.
Before the **24th Amendment**, the **President** had the discretion to either give or withhold his assent to a **Constitution Amendment Bill** passed by **Parliament**.
However, the **24th Amendment**, which was enacted in **1971**, substituted the phrase **shall give his assent** for the word **may,** thereby making it **mandatory** for the **President** to give his assent to a **Constitution Amendment Bill** once it has been duly passed by both Houses of **Parliament**.
**

Important Tips-The **23rd Amendment** of the Constitution of India, enacted in **1969**, extended the **reservation of seats** for **Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes** and representation of **Anglo-Indians** by nomination in the **Lok Sabha** and the **State Legislative Assemblies** for another ten years, i.e., up to **1980**
- The **42nd Amendment** of the Constitution of India, enacted in **1976**, gave **primacy to the Directive Principles** over **Fundamental Rights**, curtailed the power of **judicial review**, strengthened the power of **Parliament** and the executive, declared India as a **sovereign socialist secular democratic republic**, added new **fundamental duties** and a new chapter on **emergency provisions**.
- The **44th Amendment** of the Constitution of India, enacted in **1978**, restored the **civil liberties and democratic rights** that were curtailed by the **42nd Amendment** during the **Emergency**.
- It also reduced the duration of **Presidents rule**, introduced **safeguards** against misuse of **emergency provisions**, and deleted the **right to property** from the list of **fundamental rights**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
I. Through the 44th Constitutional Amendment, 1978 all the Directive Principles of State Policy have been given preference over Fundamental Rights mentioned in Articles 14 and 19.

II. Supreme Court has laid down in its verdict in Minerva Mills Case, 1980 that the preference given only to Directive Principles of State Policy mentioned in Article 39 (b) and 39 (c) over Fundamental Rights mentioned in Articles 14 and 19 is Constitutional.
Code:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: I. Through the 44th Constitutional Amendment, 1978 all the Directive Principles of State Policy have been given preference over Fundamental Rights mentioned in Articles 14 and 19. II. Supreme Court has laid down in its verdict in Minerva Mills Case, 1980 that the preference given only to Directive Principles of State Policy mentioned in Article 39 (b) and 39 (c) over Fundamental Rights mentioned in Articles 14 and 19 is Constitutional. Code:

options.a: Neither I nor II is correct.
options.d: Both I and II are correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement I is incorrect: 44th Constitutional Amendment,**1978**did not give preference to all the Directive Principles of State Policy over Fundamental Rights mentioned in Articles 14 and 19. It only retained the preference given to Directive Principles contained in Article 39 (b) and (c) by the 25th Amendment,**1971**.**

**Statement II is correct: Supreme Court has laid down in its verdict in Minerva Mills Case,**1980**that the preference given only to Directive Principles of State Policy mentioned in Article 39 (b) and (c) over Fundamental Rights mentioned in Articles 14 and 19 is Constitutional. **Important Tips** Minerva Mill Case:
- The case was filed by Minerva Mills, a textile company that was nationalized by the government under the Sick Textile Undertakings Act, **1974**.
- The case challenged the validity of clauses 4 and 5 of Article 368, which were inserted by the 42nd Amendment Act, **1976**. These clauses gave unlimited power to Parliament to amend any part of the Constitution, including the basic structure, and excluded judicial review of such amendments.
- The Supreme Court struck down these clauses as unconstitutional and violative of the basic structure doctrine.
- The court held that Parliaments power to amend is limited by the Constitution and cannot be used to destroy its identity and spirit.
- The court also upheld the validity of clause 1 of Article 31C, which gave preference to Directive Principles of State Policy contained in Article 39 (b) and (c) over Fundamental Rights mentioned in Articles 14 and 19. However, the court struck down clause 2 of Article 31C, which immunized any law from judicial scrutiny even if it does not give effect to the Directive Principles.
- The court reaffirmed the doctrine of basic structure and harmonious construction between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles.
- The court also clarified that the basic structure includes the limited amending power of Parliament, the supremacy of the Constitution, the democratic and secular character of the polity, the separation of powers, and the individual freedom.

options.c: Stringent measures to deal with terrorism
options.d: Provision of Lokpal to prevent corruption

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The 97th Amendment of the Constitution of India, officially known as The Constitution (Ninety-seventh Amendment) Act, **2011**, introduced clauses dealing with the organization and working of cooperative societies in the country.
**Important Tips** Features of 97th Constitutional Amendment Act:
- It inserted a new Part IXB in the Constitution, which contains Articles 243ZH to 243ZT, which deal with various aspects of cooperative societies such as incorporation, membership, elections, audit, supervision, offences and penalties.
- The amendment also added the words or co-operative societies to Article 19(1)(c) of the Constitution to expand the fundamental right to form associations or unions to cover cooperative societies too.
- It also added a new Directive Principle under Article 43B, which says: The State shall endeavour to promote voluntary formation, autonomous functioning, democratic control and professional management of co-operative societies

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which of the following is not correctly matched?
Provisions - Sources
1. Article 22(1) - Right to be defended by a legal practitioner of her or his choice.
2. Article 22(4) - No law providing preventive detention shall authorize the detention for a period longer than three months.
3. Article 22(2) - Parliament may by law prescribe the circumstances under which a person may be detained for a period longer than three months.
4. Article 22(1) - No person who is arrested shall be in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such an arrest.
questions.questionText: Which of the following is not correctly matched? Provisions - Sources 1. Article 22(1) - Right to be defended by a legal practitioner of her or his choice. 2. Article 22(4) - No law providing preventive detention shall authorize the detention for a period longer than three months. 3. Article 22(2) - Parliament may by law prescribe the circumstances under which a person may be detained for a period longer than three months. 4. Article 22(1) - No person who is arrested shall be in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such an arrest.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Option (c) Article 22(2) is not correctly matched. The correct match for Article 22(2) is: Every person who is arrested and detained in custody shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of twenty-four hours of such arrest excluding the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the court of the magistrate and no such person shall be detained in custody beyond the said period without the authority of a magistrate.
**Important Tips** Article 22 is divided into six clauses, which are:
- Clause (1): Right to be informed of the grounds for arrest and the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of choice.
- Clause (2): Detained individuals must be produced before the nearest magistrate within 24 hours, and their detention cannot exceed this period without the authority of a magistrate.
- Clause (3): Exemptions to the rights of arrest and detention under clauses (1) and (2) for enemy aliens and those detained under laws providing for preventive detention.
- Clause (4): Preventive detention laws cannot authorize detention for more than three months unless an Advisory Board of High Court judges reports sufficient cause for the continued detention.
- Clause (5): Detained individuals must be provided with the grounds for the order of preventive detention and given an opportunity to make a representation against it.
- Clause (6): The authority making the order of preventive detention is not obligated to disclose facts that it deems against the public interest.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Under which of the following Article of the Supreme Court is empowered to issue different 'Writs' for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights of the citizens?
questions.questionText: Under which of the following Article of the Supreme Court is empowered to issue different 'Writs' for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights of the citizens?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Article 32 of the Constitution of India empowers the Supreme Court to issue different writs for the enforcement of fundamental rights of the citizens.
A writ is a written order from the court that commands constitutional remedies for the violation or protection of the right. The Supreme Court can issue five types of writs, namely, habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, certiorari and quo warranto.
Article 32 is considered as a part of the basic structure of the Constitution and cannot be suspended except during an emergency.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Which Constitutional Amendment bifurcated the Combined National Commission for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes into two separate bodies?
questions.questionText: Which Constitutional Amendment bifurcated the Combined National Commission for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes into two separate bodies?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The 89th Amendment of the Constitution of India, officially known as The Constitution (Eighty-ninth Amendment) Act, **2003**, bifurcated the combined National Commission for SCs and STs into two separate bodies, namely, National Commission for Scheduled Castes (under Article 338) and National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (under Article 338-A).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: As a part of the Right to Freedom,
which one of the following does not form part of "Freedom to Assemble Peaceably and Without Arms"?
questions.questionText: As a part of the Right to Freedom, which one of the following does not form part of "Freedom to Assemble Peaceably and Without Arms"?

options.a: Gherao officials not performing their duties
options.c: People who assemble should not bear arms
options.d: State can make a law imposing reasonable restrictions on the exercise of this Right

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Gherao is a form of protest or agitation in which a group of people surround an official or a building and prevent them from leaving or entering until their demands are met.
Gherao is not protected by Article 19 (1) (b) of the Constitution. It is punishable under various sections of the Indian Penal Code, such as Section 141 (unlawful assembly), Section 143 (punishment for unlawful assembly), Section 147 (rioting), Section 149 (common object of unlawful assembly), Section 341 (wrongful restraint), Section 342 (wrongful confinement), Section 353 (assault or criminal force to deter public servant from discharge of his duty), etc.

questions.subject: Polity

statementLines: Which of the following constitution (Amendment) Act provides for goods and Services Tax (G.S.T.)?
questions.questionText: Which of the following constitution (Amendment) Act provides for goods and Services Tax (G.S.T.)?

options.c: The Constitutions (One-Hundredth and First) Amendment Act 2015
options.d: The Constitutions (One Hundred and First) Amendment Act 2016

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Constitutions (One Hundred and First) Amendment Act **2016** introduced a national Goods and Services Tax (GST) in India from 1 July **2017**. The amendment gave concurrent taxing powers to the Union and the States to levy GST on every transaction of supply of goods or services or both.
It also created the GST Council, a joint body of the Centre and States, to recommend matters related to GST.

questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: The rights are called Fundamental Rights because
i. It is written in the Constitution.
ii. It is democratic.
iii. It is public welfare.
iv. It is essential for personality development.
v. Parliament can't make law against it.
Code:
questions.questionText: The rights are called Fundamental Rights because i. It is written in the Constitution. ii. It is democratic. iii. It is public welfare. iv. It is essential for personality development. v. Parliament can't make law against it. Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The rights are called Fundamental Rights because:
- i. It is written in the Constitution. The Fundamental Rights are enshrined in Part III of the Constitution of India from Article 12 to 35 and are guaranteed to all citizens.
- iv. It is essential for personality development. The Fundamental Rights are defined as basic human freedoms where every Indian citizen has the right to enjoy for a proper and harmonious development of personality and life.
- v. Parliament cant make law against it. The Fundamental Rights are enforceable by the courts, subject to certain restrictions, and any law made by the Parliament that violates or abridges the Fundamental Rights can be declared unconstitutional by the judiciary-vi. Although the Fundamental Rights are essential for a democratic system, they are not exclusively democratic in nature. There are other forms of government that may also grant some civil liberties to their citizens, such as constitutional monarchy or republicanism.
- vii. The Fundamental Rights are not necessarily related to public welfare, which is more of a goal of the Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV of the Constitution). The Fundamental Rights are more concerned with individual rights and liberties that protect the citizens from arbitrary state action or discrimination.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: What is correct about external emergency as per Article 352?
i. It is proclaimed by the President.
ii. It should be approved by Parliament within two months.
iii. It should be approved by Parliament within one month.
iv. It should be approved by Parliament by a simple majority.
v. It should be approved by Parliament by a 2/3 majority.
vi. It can be revoked by a simple majority of Parliament.
vii. It can be revoked by a 2/3 majority of Parliament.
viii. It can be approved in a joint session of Parliament.
Code:
questions.questionText: What is correct about external emergency as per Article 352? i. It is proclaimed by the President. ii. It should be approved by Parliament within two months. iii. It should be approved by Parliament within one month. iv. It should be approved by Parliament by a simple majority. v. It should be approved by Parliament by a 2/3 majority. vi. It can be revoked by a simple majority of Parliament. vii. It can be revoked by a 2/3 majority of Parliament. viii. It can be approved in a joint session of Parliament. Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. When a national emergency is declared on the ground of war or external aggression, it is known as External Emergency.
On the other hand, when it is declared on the ground of armed rebellion, it is known as Internal Emergency. As per Article 352, the President can declare a national emergency when the security of India is threatened by war/external aggression/armed rebellion.
The proclamation of Emergency must be approved by both the Houses of Parliament within one month. If approved by both the Houses of Parliament, the emergency continues for six months.
For the proclamation of emergency or its continuance, the resolution must be passed by either House of Parliament by a special majority. A revocation of emergency does not require parliamentary approval. It can be revoked by the President at any time by a subsequent proclamation.
The emergency must be revoked if the Lok Sabha passes a resolution by a simple majority disapproving of its continuation. **Important Tips**
- President can declare a national emergency even before the actual occurrence of these situations if he is satisfied that there is a probable danger.
- The emergency can be extended to an indefinite period with an approval of Parliament every six months.
- The President can proclaim a national emergency only after receiving a written recommendation from the cabinet

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: By which of the following grounds, a citizen's freedom of expression may not be subjected to restriction?
questions.questionText: By which of the following grounds, a citizen's freedom of expression may not be subjected to restriction?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Freedom of expression is a fundamental right guaranteed by Article 19 (1) (a) of the Constitution of India.
It includes the right to express ones views and opinions through any mode of communication, such as words, signs, symbols, gestures, pictures, etc. It also includes the right to receive and impart information from any source and disseminate it to others.
However, freedom of expression is not absolute or unlimited. It is subject to reasonable restrictions imposed by the State under Article 19 (2) of the Constitution.
These restrictions can be imposed in the interests of the sovereignty and integrity of India, the security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States, public order, decency or morality, contempt of court, defamation or incitement to an offence.
Unbecoming criticism is not one of the grounds on which freedom of expression can be restricted. Unbecoming criticism means criticism that is inappropriate, improper, or disrespectful.
However, such criticism may not necessarily violate any of the grounds mentioned in Article 19 (2) of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: The Provisions regarding division of taxes between the Union and the States-
questions.questionText: The Provisions regarding division of taxes between the Union and the States-

options.a: Can be suspended during Financial Emergency
options.b: Can be suspended during National Emergency
options.c: Cannot be suspended under any circumstances
options.d: Can be suspended only with the consent of a majority of the State Legislatures

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. During National Emergency the President by order can direct that all provisions regarding division of taxes between Union and States and grants-in-aids remain suspended.
However, such suspension shall not go beyond the expiration of the financial year in which the Proclamation ceases to operate.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: By which Constitutional Amendment OBCs have been given 27 percent reservation in the admission to educational institutions
questions.questionText: By which Constitutional Amendment OBCs have been given 27 percent reservation in the admission to educational institutions
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The 93rd Amendment of the Constitution of India, officially known as The Constitution (Ninety-third Amendment) Act, **2005**, added clause (5) in Article 15 which enables the government to make any special provision for the advancement of any socially and educationally backward classes of citizens or for the Scheduled Castes or the Scheduled Tribes in so far as such special provisions relate to their admission to educational institutions, including private educational institutions, whether aided or unaided by the State, other than the minority educational institutions.
The amendment enabled the implementation of the 27% reservation for OBCs in admission to educational institutions as recommended by the Mandal Commission in **1992**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which Article of the Constitution provides protection to the accused regarding conviction from double-bar and self-incrimination?
questions.questionText: Which Article of the Constitution provides protection to the accused regarding conviction from double-bar and self-incrimination?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Article 20 of the Constitution of India is a fundamental right that provides protection to the accused regarding conviction from double-bar (double jeopardy) and self-incrimination.
The article is divided into three clauses, which are:
- Clause (1): Protection against ex post facto laws. It means that no person shall be convicted of any offence except for violation of a law in force at the time of the commission of the act, nor be subjected to a penalty greater than that prescribed by such law.
- Clause (2): Protection against double jeopardy. It means that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once.
- Clause (3): Protection against self-incrimination. It means that no person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: The Constitution of India does not guarantee
which one of the following as a fundamental right?
questions.questionText: The Constitution of India does not guarantee which one of the following as a fundamental right?

options.d: Right to carry on trade and business

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The Constitution of India does not guarantee the Right to Property as a fundamental right. Originally, the right to property was included as a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31 of the Constitution.
However, the 44th Amendment Act of **1978** removed the right to property from the list of fundamental rights and made it a legal right instead.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: Which one of the following statements with regard to the Directive Principles of State Policy is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements with regard to the Directive Principles of State Policy is not correct?

options.a: They are not enforceable in a court of law.
options.b: They can override all the fundamental rights.
options.c: They can be related to social and economic justice.
options.d: They can be related to giving free legal aid.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The Directive Principles of State Policy cannot override fundamental rights. Fundamental rights, enshrined in Part III of the Indian Constitution, are the individual rights and liberties that are guaranteed to every citizen.
If a conflict arises between a fundamental right and a directive principle, the courts generally strive to interpret the laws in a way that reconciles both, but in cases where reconciliation is not possible, the fundamental rights take precedence.
**Important Tips**
- DPSP are a set of guidelines or principles provided in Part IV of the Constitution of India.
- They serve as moral and political directives for the government in formulating policies and making laws.
- DPSP are not legally enforceable in a court of law.
- They are non-justiciable, meaning they cannot be directly enforced through the courts.
- The DPSP aim to establish social and economic justice in society.
- They provide a framework for the government to promote the welfare of the people, secure a just and humane society, and reduce inequalities.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which one of the following fundamental rights has not been provided to a person?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following fundamental rights has not been provided to a person?

options.a: Protection against prosecution and punishment for the same offence more than once.
options.b: To refuse to give his/her sample of handwriting as evidence to support a prosecution against him/her.
options.c: To act as a witness against himself/herself.
options.d: Right not to be convicted of any offence except for violation of a law in force at the time of commission of the act charged as an offenc

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. According to the Article 20 (3) of the Indian Constitution, No person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.
This indicates that a person has the right to not be compelled to act as a witness against themselves. **Important Tips** Some points about Article 20 of the Indian Constitution:
- It safeguards against arbitrary and excessive punishment for accused persons, including citizens, foreigners, and legal entities such as companies or corporations.
- No ex-post-facto law: It ensures that a person cannot be convicted of any offence except for the violation of a law in force at the time of committing the act.
- No double jeopardy: No person can be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once.
- No self-incrimination: No person accused of any offence can be compelled to be a witness against themselves.
- An ex-post-facto law is one that imposes penalties retrospectively, which means it applies to acts that were committed before the law was enacted or increases the penalties for such acts.
- Article 20 prohibits the enactment of ex-post-facto criminal laws, but this limitation does not apply to civil laws or tax laws.
Civil liability or taxes can be imposed retrospectively.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: In relation to the fundamental duties enshrined in the Constitution of India,
which one of the following is NOT correct?
questions.questionText: In relation to the fundamental duties enshrined in the Constitution of India, which one of the following is NOT correct?

options.a: The fundamental duties were not originally a part of the Constitution
options.b: To safeguard public property and abjure violence are fundamental duties
options.c: They are not enforceable through writs
options.d: They may not be used for determining constitutionality of laws

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. While fundamental duties do not directly determine the constitutionality of laws, they are often considered by the courts while interpreting and deciding cases related to the fundamental rights of citizens.
Fundamental duties provide guidance and context in interpreting the fundamental rights, and they can influence the courts decision-making process.
**Important Tips**
- The fundamental duties were added to the Constitution of India through the 42nd Amendment Act in **1976** through Article 51A of the Constitution of India.
- The fundamental duties are not just applicable to citizens but also to the State and every individual residing in India.
- The fundamental duties aim to promote patriotism, unity, and the spirit of common brotherhood among citizens.
- Some of the fundamental duties include respecting the Constitution and its ideals, promoting harmony and the spirit of inquiry, safeguarding public property, protecting the environment, and striving towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity.
- The fundamental duties are non-enforceable through writs.
- The fundamental duties are not justiciable, meaning individuals cannot be legally prosecuted for failing to fulfill their fundamental duties.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: "To cherish and follow the noble ideas which inspired our national struggle for freedom" is a provision of
which one of the following of the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: "To cherish and follow the noble ideas which inspired our national struggle for freedom" is a provision of which one of the following of the Constitution of India?

options.a: Directive Principles of the State Policy

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The provision "To cherish and follow the noble ideas which inspired our national struggle for freedom is a part of the Fundamental Duties (Art 51A-b) mentioned in the Constitution of India.

questions.subject: Polity

statementLines: Which of the following is NOT provided for under the Directive Principles of State Policy?
questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT provided for under the Directive Principles of State Policy?

options.a: Right to work, to education and to public assistance in certain cases
options.b: Uniform Civil Code for the citizens
options.c: Separation of Judiciary from the executive
options.d: Participation of workers in the management of local-self government

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Participation of workers has been provided for under DPSPs (Art 43A), but it is for the management of industries and not for local self-government.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
statementLines: Which one of the following statements regarding the Directive Principles of State Policy is NOT correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements regarding the Directive Principles of State Policy is NOT correct?

options.a: State shall follow the Directive Principles of State Policy both in the matter of administration as well as in the making of laws
options.b: The Directive Principles of State Policy embody the object of the State under the Republican Constitution
options.c: The Directive Principles of State Policy have precedence over the Fundamental Rights in case of conflict between the two
options.d: The Directive Principles of State Policy are not enforceable in the Courts.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. In the Minerva Mills case (**1980**), the Supreme Court of India examined the relationship between the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP) and the Fundamental Rights enshrined in the Indian Constitution.
The court held that the DPSP and the Fundamental Rights are both equally important and essential for the governance of the country. They are complementary to each other and must be interpreted harmoniously.

The Act was passed on 26 April **1975**, and Sikkim became an Associate State on 16 May **1975**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: Which one of the following concerns has found a place in both Fundamental Duties and Directive Principles of State Policy?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following concerns has found a place in both Fundamental Duties and Directive Principles of State Policy?

options.b: Protection of improvement of environment, forests and wildlife
options.c: Respect for the rich heritage of our composite culture

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Both the Fundamental Duties and Directive Principles of State Policy recognize and emphasize the concern for the protection and improvement of the environment, forests, and wildlife.
**Important Tips**
- Fundamental Duty: Article 51 A(g) of the Constitution of India states that it shall be the fundamental duty of every citizen of India to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife.
- Directive Principle: Article 48A of the Constitution of India states that the State shall endeavour to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country.

questions.subject: Polity

options.c: Duty of the State to raise the level of nutrition and standard of living
options.d: Promotion of scientific temper, humanity and the spirit of inquiry and reform

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Promotion of scientific temper, humanity and the spirit of inquiry and reform is actually a part of the Fundamental Duties and not DPSP. Article 51A(h) of the Indian Constitution states that it shall be the duty of every citizen to develop scientific temper, humanism, and the spirit of inquiry and reform.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: The power of the President of India to refer a matter back to the Council of Ministers for reconsideration was inserted in the Constitution by
questions.questionText: The power of the President of India to refer a matter back to the Council of Ministers for reconsideration was inserted in the Constitution by
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The 44th Amendment Act added a new clause to Article 74 of the Constitution, which gives the President the power to refer a matter back to the Council of Ministers for reconsideration.
This power can be exercised by the President only once on a matter. If the Council of Ministers sends the same advice again to the President, the President must accept it.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
statementLines: The provision under Article-51A of the Constitution of India relates to the
questions.questionText: The provision under Article-51A of the Constitution of India relates to the

options.a: Uniform civil code for the citizens

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Article 51A of the Constitution of India deals with the fundamental duties of citizens. It lists a set of moral, civic, and ethical obligations that citizens are expected to fulfill for the betterment of society and the nation as a whole.
The fundamental duties were added to the Constitution by the 42nd Amendment Act in **1976**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: The amending power of the Parliament set forth in
which one of the following Articles of the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: The amending power of the Parliament set forth in which one of the following Articles of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The amending power of the Parliament is set forth in Article 368 of the Constitution of India.
Article 368 provides the procedure for amending the Constitution, including the types of amendments, the majority required for their passage, and the involvement of the President in the amendment process.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: With regard to a Constitution Amendment Bill,
which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.questionText: With regard to a Constitution Amendment Bill, which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.a: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha can call a joint sitting of both the Houses to pass the Bill
options.b: Each House needs to pass the Bill separately by a prescribed special majority end
options.c: The Bill can be introduced in either House of Parliament
options.d: The Bill can be sponsored by a Private Member

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.

Option a is incorrect: The Speaker of Lok Sabha cannot call a joint sitting of both Houses to pass a Constitution Amendment Bill, as such a bill must be passed separately by each House with the required majority.
Option b is correct: A Constitution Amendment Bill must be passed separately by both the Houses of Parliament with a special majority. This means that it should be supported by a majority of the total membership of each House, as well as by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.
Option c is correct: A Constitution Amendment Bill can be introduced in either House of Parliament by a minister or a private member, and does not require the prior approval of the President

Option d is correct: A Constitution Amendment Bill can be sponsored by a private member, but it has very little chance of being passed by Parliament.

This means that both citizens and foreigners are entitled to the protection of Article 14 and it does not apply to Indian citizens living abroad and citizens born in India but living abroad.

questions.questionText: Which of the following are provided in India by the Right to Education Act? 1. Right of children to free and compulsory education till completion of elementary education in a neighborhood school. 2. Prohibition of deployment of teachers for non-educational works, other than decennial census, elections to local authority like State Legislatures, Parliament and disaster relief. 3. Right of minorities to establish and administer education institution. 4. No citizen shall be denied admission into any educational institution maintained by the State or receiving aid out of State funds on grounds only of religion, race, caste, language or any of them. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: The Right to Education Act guarantees the right of children to receive free and compulsory education until the completion of elementary education.** It emphasizes the importance of providing education to all children and ensures that they have access to education in their neighborhood.
**Statement 2 is correct: The Act prohibits the deployment of teachers for non-educational works, except for specific purposes such as the decennial census, elections, and disaster relief.** This provision aims to ensure that teachers are focused on their primary role of imparting education.

**Statement 3 is incorrect: The Right to Education Act does not specifically provide for the right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions.** This provision is covered under Article 30 of the Constitution of India, which guarantees the right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice.
**Statement 4 is incorrect: Article 29(2) of the Constitution of India and not RTE Act prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, language or any of them in admission to educational institutions.**

Free and compulsory education for all children of the age of 6-14 yr. 2. Prohibition of trafficking in human beings and forced labour. 3. Promotion of the educational and economic interests of the weaker sections of the people, especially the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes. Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: Free and compulsory education for all children of the age of 6-14 years, relates to the Fundamental Right to Education.** This right is provided under Article 21A of the Constitution of India.

**Statement 2 is correct: The prohibition of trafficking in human beings and forced labor, relates to the Fundamental Right against exploitation.** This right is provided under Article 23 of the Constitution of India.
**Statement 3 is incorrect: Improvement of public health and prohibition of intoxicating drinks, does not directly relate to any specific Fundamental Right as enshrined in the Constitution of India.** However, the Constitution does provide for the promotion of public health as a directive principle of state policy under Article 47.
**Statement 4 is incorrect: Promotion of the educational and economic interests of the weaker sections of the people, especially the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes is provided under Article 46 of DPSP.**

statementLines: Which of the following are related to Right to Equality under the provisions of Articles 14-18 of the Constitution of India?

questions.questionText: Which of the following are related to Right to Equality under the provisions of Articles 14-18 of the Constitution of India? 1. Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth. 2. Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment. 3. Educational and cultural rights to minorities. 4. Abolition of untouchability. Select the correct answer using the codes given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: The right to equality, as enshrined in Articles 14-18 of the Constitution of India, prohibits discrimination on the basis of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth (Article 15).** It ensures that every citizen of India is treated equally before the law.

**Statement 2 is correct: The right to equality includes the principle of equality of opportunity in matters of public employment (Article 16).** It ensures that all individuals have an equal opportunity to be considered for public employment based on their qualifications and abilities, without discrimination.
**Statement 3 is incorrect: The right to equality, under the provisions of Articles 14-18, does not specifically grant educational and cultural rights to minorities.** They are part of the Cultural and Educational Rights under Articles 29-30 of the Constitution.
**Statement 4 is correct: The right to equality includes the abolition of untouchability.** Untouchability, which was a practice associated with the caste system in India, was abolished under Article 17 of the Constitution.
It ensures that every individual is treated with dignity and without any form of untouchability.

There is often a debate and tension between these two categories of rights, as they require different approaches and resource allocation. Some argue that civil and political rights are fundamental and must be prioritized, while others emphasize the importance of economic, social, and cultural rights for achieving social justice and equality.

options.a: To render national service when called upon to do so
options.b: To renounce practices derogatory to the dignity of women
options.c: To value and preserve a unitary national culture
options.d: To develop the spirit of inquiry and reform

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. To value and preserve a unitary national culture is not a Fundamental Duty as mentioned in Part-IVA of the Constitution of India.
However, the other options, are all mentioned as duties of every citizen of India. These duties aim to promote the welfare of society, gender equality, and the pursuit of knowledge and progress.
**Important Tips**
- The Indian Constitutions Fundamental Duties draw inspiration from the former USSRs Constitution, added through the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, **1976**, based on the Swaran Singh Committees recommendation, and are non-justiciable obligations applicable to citizens only.
- List of ten Fundamental Duties:
- To abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals and institutions.
- To cherish and follow the noble ideals that inspired the national struggle for freedom.
- To uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity, and integrity of India.
- To defend the country and render national service when required.
- To promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood among all citizens.
- To value and preserve the rich heritage of Indias composite culture.
- To protect and improve the natural environment, including forests, lakes, rivers, and wildlife.
- To develop scientific temper, humanism, and the spirit of inquiry and reform.
- To safeguard public property and abjure violence.
- To strive for excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity.

**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The term Audi Alteram Partem is a Latin phrase that translates to hear the other side. statementLines: Which one of the following statements about a Bill for Amendment of the Constitution of India is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements about a Bill for Amendment of the Constitution of India is not correct?

options.a: It is governed by Article 368(2) of the Constitution of India.
options.b: Joint sitting can be resorted to for Passing a Bill amending the Constitution of India.
options.c: The State Legislatures cannot initiate any Bill or proposal for amendment of the Constitution of India.
options.d: The previous sanction of the President of India is not required for introducing any Bill in the Parliament for amendment of the Constitution of India.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. A joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament is not allowed in the case of a deadlock over the passage of a constitutional amendment bill.
However, in the case of an ordinary bill, there is a provision for a joint sitting if needed. **Important Tips**
- The procedure for amending the Indian Constitution was borrowed from the South Africa Constitution.
- Article 368 in Part XX of the Constitution deals with the powers of Parliament to amend the Constitution and its procedure.
- Parliament can amend the constitution provided that Basic Structure remains intact-Kesvananda Bharti Case **1973**.
- Parliament can only initiate constitutional amendment bill.
- It can be introduced by either minister or private member-does not require president approval.
- The bill must be passed by each house by Special Majority.
- The President must give his assent-Can not return or withhold assent of the constitutional amendment bill.
- Three ways in which constitution can be amended:
- Simple Majority of the parliament.
- Special Majority of the Parliament.
- Special Majority of the Parliament and the ratification of half of the state legislatures

statementLines: Which one of the following statements is correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is correct?

options.a: Under Article 21A of the Constitution of India, the State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children up to the age of eleven years.
options.b: Every religious denomination has got absolute power under Article 26 of the Constitution of India to manage its own affairs in matters of religion.
options.c: Only religious and linguistic minorities find mention in Article 30 of the Constitution of India in reference to the right to establish educational institutions of their choice.
options.d: Parliament cannot empower, even by law, any court other than the Supreme Court of India the power to issue writs within local limits of its jurisdiction.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

Option a is incorrect: **Article 21A** guarantees **free and compulsory education** to all children in the age group of **six to fourteen years**, not eleven years.
Option b is incorrect: While **Article 26** grants religious denominations the right to **manage their own affairs in matters of religion**, this power is **not absolute**. It is subject to **public order, morality, and health**, and is also subject to other provisions of the Constitution, including **fundamental rights**.
Option c is correct: **Article 30** of the Constitution of India guarantees the right to establish and administer educational institutions to **religious and linguistic minorities**. It states that all **religious and linguistic minorities** have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice, whether aided or unaided by the state.
Option d is incorrect: **Parliament** has the power to empower **other courts**, besides the Supreme Court, to issue **writs**. The power to issue writs is **not exclusive to the Supreme Court** and can be conferred on high courts or other courts by law.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **Article 21 A**, which guarantees the **Right to Education**, was inserted into the Constitution of India through the **86th Amendment Act**. This amendment added **Article 21A**, which states that the State shall provide **free and compulsory education** to all children in the age group of **six to fourteen years**.

questions.questionText: Equality before the law or equal protection of the laws within the territory of India is guaranteed under which one of the following Articles of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The guarantee of **equality before the law** or **equal protection of the laws** within the territory of India is provided under **Article 14** of the Constitution of India. **Article 14** of the Indian Constitution states that The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India. This article forms a **fundamental right** and ensures that all individuals, irrespective of their **caste, religion, gender, race, or place of birth**, are treated equally and without discrimination under the law.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
statementLines: Which one among the following was eliminated by the 44th Amendment Act of the Parliament?
questions.questionText: Which one among the following was eliminated by the 44th Amendment Act of the Parliament?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **Right to Property** was originally included as a **fundamental right** under **Article 19(1)(f)** of the Indian Constitution, which guaranteed the right to **acquire, hold, and dispose of property**. However, through the **44th Amendment Act**, passed in **1978**, the **Right to Property** was removed as a fundamental right and was instead reclassified as a **legal right**.
**

Important Tips Some other changes under **44th Amendment Act**:
- **Proclamation of Emergency**: **Proclamation of Emergency** can be issued only when the **security of India** or any part of its territory is threatened by **war or external aggression or by armed rebellion**. **Internal disturbance** not amounting to armed rebellion would not be a ground for the issue of a Proclamation. An emergency can be proclaimed only on the basis of **written advice** tendered to the President by the **Cabinet**.
- **Judicial Review**: The amendment reinforced the power of **judicial review** by stating that **Parliament** cannot amend the Constitution in a way that would take away or abridge the **basic structure** of the Constitution.
- **Fundamental Rights during Emergency**: The amendment clarified that certain **fundamental rights**, including the **right to life and personal liberty**, cannot be suspended even during a state of emergency.
- Restored the original terms of **Lok sabha** and the **state legislative assemblies** (i.e. 5 years).

**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect:**Freedom of speech**, as guaranteed by the Constitution of India, is available**only to citizens of India**under**Article 19**.**

**Statement 2 is correct:**Protection against self-incrimination**is available to**both citizens and non-citizens**under**Article 20**.**

**Statement 3 is correct:**Freedom of conscience**is available to**both citizens and non-citizens**under**Article 25**.**Freedom of conscience**protects an individuals right to profess, practice, or propagate any**religion or belief**.**

**Statement 4 is incorrect:**Non-discrimination in matters of employment**is specifically guaranteed**only to citizens of India**under**Article 16**. 

Important Tips: Fundamental Rights **Available Only to Citizens of India**:
- **Article 15**
- Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of **religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth**.
- **Article 16**
- **Equality of opportunity** in matters of public employment.
- **Article 19**
- Protection of **six rights** related to freedom
- (a) of **speech and expression**; (b) to **assemble peaceably and without arms**; (c) to **form associations or unions**; (d) to **move freely throughout the territory of India**; (e) to **reside and settle in any part of the territory of India**; and (f) to **practice any profession, or to carry on any occupation, trade or business**.
- **Article 29**
- Protection of **language, script and culture of minorities**.
- **Article 30**
- Right of **minorities** to establish and administer **educational institutions**.

questions.questionText: The provision, "the State shall take steps to separate the Judiciary from the Executive in the public services of the State" is incorporated in which part of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The Constitution of India includes provisions in **Part IV (Article 36-51)** known as the **Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP)**. One such principle is highlighted in **Article 50**, which emphasizes the importance of **separating the judiciary from the executive** in the public services of the State. This provision reflects the commitment to maintaining the **independence and impartiality of the judiciary**, ensuring a fair and just system of governance.
**

Important Tips Classification of **Directive Principles of State Policy**:
- **Socialistic Principles**
- **Article 38, 39, 39A, 41, 42, 43, 43A, 47**
- **Gandhian Principles**
- **Article 40, 43, 43B, 46, 47, 48**
- **Liberal-Intellectual Principles**
- **Article 44, 45, 48, 48A, 49, 50, and 51**.
The Constitution **does not contain any classification** of Directive Principles. It is classified on the basis of their **content and direction**.

statementLines: Which one of the following is the correct meaning of the term 'State', as defined in Article 12 of the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is the correct meaning of the term 'State', as defined in Article 12 of the Constitution of India?

options.a: It refers only to the Government of India.
options.b: It refers only to the Government of India and the Parliament of India.
options.c: It refers only to the Government of India, Parliament of India, and Governments and Legislatures of each State.
options.d: It refers to the Government of India, Parliament of India, Governments and Legislatures of each State, and all local or other authorities within the Territory of India.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Article 12** of the Constitution of India defines the term **State** for the purpose of interpreting **fundamental rights**. According to **Article 12**, the term **State** includes not only the **Government of India and the Parliament of India** but also the **Governments and Legislatures of each State**.
**

Important Tips: According to **Article 12**, **State** includes:
- **Government and Parliament of India**
- executive and legislative organs of the Union government.
- **Government and legislature of states**
- executive and legislative organs of state government.
- **All Local Authorities**
- municipalities, panchayats, district boards, improvement trusts, etc.
- **All Other Authorities**
- statutory or non-statutory authorities like **LIC, ONGC, SAIL**, etc.
- A **private body or an agency** working as an instrument of the State falls within the meaning of the **State** under **Article 12**.

statementLines: Which one of the following is not a circumstance for proclamation of Emergency by the President of India under Article 352 of the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not a circumstance for proclamation of Emergency by the President of India under Article 352 of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. A **national emergency** in India can be declared based on the grounds of **war, external aggression, or armed rebellion**. The term **armed rebellion** was introduced through the **44th amendment** to the Constitution. Prior to this amendment, it was referred to as **internal disturbance. 

Important Tips:
- A **national emergency** in India can be proclaimed under **Article 352** of the Constitution during situations of **war, external aggression, or armed rebellion** that poses a threat to the countrys security and integrity.
- The **President of India** has the authority to declare a national emergency based on the advice of the **Union Cabinet**.
- Once a national emergency is proclaimed, the **central government** assumes additional powers, and the **federal structure** of the country undergoes a temporary change.
- **Fundamental rights** guaranteed by the Constitution can be suspended or curtailed during a national emergency, except for certain rights mentioned in **Article 20 and Article 21**.
- The proclamation of a national emergency must be approved by **both houses of Parliament within one month** and is valid for **six months**. It can be extended with the approval of **Parliament** in subsequent intervals of **six months**.

The Constitution guarantees this fundamental right while allowing necessary limitations for **public safety**.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following Articles of the Constitution of India protects a person against double jeopardy?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Article-20** of the Constitution of India protects a person against **double jeopardy**. **Double jeopardy** refers to the principle that an individual **cannot be prosecuted or punished more than once for the same offense**. The article states three specific protections:
- **No Double Jeopardy**.
- **No ex-post facto law**.
- **No self-incrimination**.
**

Important Tips: **Double Jeopardy**
- **Double jeopardy** protection is applicable **only in legal proceedings before a court or judicial tribunal**. It **does not apply to proceedings before departmental or administrative authorities** since they are not of a judicial nature.
**No ex-post facto law**
- An **ex-post-facto law** refers to a law that applies punishments or increases penalties for actions that have already been committed in the past. In other words, it is a law that is enforced **retroactively**. It is applicable **only to criminal laws**.
**No Self-Incrimination**
- It means no person accused of any offence shall be **compelled to be a witness against himself**. The protection against **self-incrimination** extends to both **oral evidence and documentary evidence**. It extends **only to criminal proceedings**. However, it **does not extend to (i) compulsory production of material objects, (ii) compulsion to give thumb impression, specimen signature, blood specimens, and (iii) compulsory exhibition of the body**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
statementLines: Which one of the following Articles was defended by Dr. BR Ambedkar on the plea that it would be used as 'a matter of last resort'?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following Articles was defended by Dr. BR Ambedkar on the plea that it would be used as 'a matter of last resort'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. During the debates of the **Constituent Assembly**, **Dr. B.R. Ambedkar**, the principal architect of the Indian Constitution, defended **Article-356** on the plea that it would be used as **a matter of last resort.** He argued that this provision would only be invoked in **exceptional circumstances** when all other remedies and alternatives have been exhausted. The intention behind **Article-356** was to ensure the maintenance of **constitutional order and stability** in the country by providing a mechanism to address critical situations in the states.
**

Important Tips:
- **Presidents Rule**, also known as **State Emergency**, is the suspension of the state government and the imposition of **direct central government rule** in a state of India.
- It is implemented under **Article 356** of the Indian Constitution when the President deems that the states governance cannot be carried out according to **constitutional provisions**.
- The decision to impose **Presidents Rule** is taken by the President on the advice of the **Governor of the concerned state or the Union Cabinet**.
- The state legislative assembly is either **dissolved or kept under suspended animation** during **Presidents Rule**.
- **Presidents Rule** can be imposed for a maximum period of **six months**, and its extension beyond six months requires **parliamentary approval** every six months.
- Currently, **Manipur** is the only state with highest number (i.e. 10) of Presidents Rule so far. **Uttar Pradesh** has 9 President Rule so far, **Bihar and Punjab** both has 8 President Rule as of today.

statementLines: What is the ground on which the Supreme Court can refuse relief under Article-32?
questions.questionText: What is the ground on which the Supreme Court can refuse relief under Article-32?

options.a: The aggrieved person can get remedy from another court.
options.b: That disputed facts have to be investigated.
options.c: That no Fundamental Right has been infringed.
options.d: That the petitioner has not asked for the proper writ applicable to his/her cas

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Under **Article-32** of the Constitution of India, individuals have the right to move the **Supreme Court** for the enforcement of their **Fundamental Rights**. However, there are certain grounds on which the **Supreme Court** can refuse relief under **Article-32**. One such ground is when it is determined that **no Fundamental Right has been infringed** in the case.

statementLines: Which one of the following Articles of the Constitution of India lays down that no citizen can be denied the use of wells, tanks and bathing ghats maintained out of state funds?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following Articles of the Constitution of India lays down that no citizen can be denied the use of wells, tanks and bathing ghats maintained out of state funds?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 15(2)** of the constitution No citizen shall, on grounds only of **religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth** or any of them, be subject to any disability, liability, restriction or condition with regard to:
- Access to shops, public restaurants, hotels and palaces of public entertainment; or-The use of **wells, tanks, bathing Ghats**, roads and places of public resort **maintained wholly or partly out of State funds** or dedicated to the use of the general public (
option b is correct). **

Important Tips:
- Referred to as the **Magna Carta** of India
- **Fundamental Rights** have been mentioned in the **Part III** of the constitution from **Article 12 to 35**.
- They are **justiciable** in nature.
- Defended and guaranteed by the **Supreme Court**.
- There are six fundamental rights recognised by the Indian constitution:
- **Right to Equality (Article 14-18)**
- **Right to Freedom (Article 19-22)**
- **Right against Exploitation (Article 23-24)**
- **Right to Freedom of Religion (Article 25-28)**
- **Cultural and Educational Rights (Article 29-30)**
- **Right to Constitutional Remedies (Article 32)**
-

NOTE: Originally, there were **7 Fundamental Rights** enshrined in the constitution. However, **Right to Property** was deleted as a fundamental right through the **44th Amendment Act, 1978**. It was made a **legal right (Article 300A)**.

statementLines: Which one of the following statements regarding freedom to manage religious affairs as per the Constitution of India is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements regarding freedom to manage religious affairs as per the Constitution of India is not correct?

options.a: Every religious denomination shall have the right to manage its own affairs in matters of religion except some minor communities.
options.b: Every religion or any section there of shall have the right to own and acquire movable and immovable property.
options.c: Every religious community has the right to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes
options.d: Every community has the right to manage its own affairs in matters of religion.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- According to **article 28** of the Indian constitution **Freedom as to attendance at religious instruction or religious worship** in certain educational institutions.
- No **religion instruction** shall be provided in any educational institution **wholly maintained out of State funds**.
- Nothing in clause (1) shall apply to an educational institution which is **administered by the State** but has been established under any **endowment or trust** which requires that religious instruction shall be imparted in such institution.
- No person attending any educational institution recognised by the State or receiving aid out of State funds shall be required to take part in any religious instruction that may be imparted in such institution or to attend any religious worship that may be conducted in such institution or in any premises attached thereto **unless such person or, if such person is a minor, his guardian has given his consent thereto** (statement a is not correct).

options.b: No citizen shall be denied admission into any educational institution maintained by the State or receiving aid out of State funds on grounds of religion, race, caste, language or any of them.
